recipes.title: Blackened Steak Salad with Blue Cheese & Blistered Tomatoes
recipes.tagline: A Cajun-crusted sirloin over cool greens, blistered tomatoes, and sharp blue cheese.
recipes.pitch: Hot steak, cold greens — the contrast is the whole point. A sirloin gets a brown-sugar-and-cayenne rub that chars into a dark, smoky crust on a screaming grill, then rests while cherry tomatoes blister in the same heat until their skins split and go jammy. Everything lands on mixed baby greens with crisp red onion, green pepper, and a balsamic-honey Dijon that you shake in a jar. *The money move: pull the steak 5°F early and let carryover finish it, so every slice stays pink against the cool leaves.*

items: ½ tsp cayenne pepper (adjust to taste)
If marinating, combine the marinade ingredients and refrigerate the steak 1–2 hours. Remove, pat very dry.
Rub the steak lightly with olive oil. Mix the rub ingredients and coat the steak evenly, pressing it in.
Whisk (or jar-shake) the dressing until emulsified. Slice the vegetables; soak the red onion in ice water for 10 minutes.
Heat the grill to high (~500°F) and oil the grates. Sear the steak 2–3 minutes per side until a dark crust forms.
Move to medium/indirect heat (~350°F) and cook to 125°F for medium-rare (it climbs to 130°F as it rests).
While the steak rests 5–10 minutes, toss the tomatoes in a little oil and blister them over high heat until the skins split and char.
Bed the greens with red onion, bell pepper, and blistered tomatoes.
Slice the rested steak thinly against the grain and fan it over the top.
Scatter blue cheese, drizzle with the dressing, and finish with cracked pepper.

tips: To find the grain, look for the parallel muscle fibers and slice perpendicular to them — short fibers mean tender bites.
Pull the steak 5°F below your target; carryover heat finishes it during the rest so it never overcooks.
Soaking the sliced red onion in ice water for 10 minutes mellows its bite and makes it crisp.
A jar with a tight lid emulsifies the dressing better than a whisk — and stores the leftovers.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Use garden cherry tomatoes for blistering and stir a pinch of chopped thyme or basil into the dressing.

extras.label: Add crunch
extras.note: Toasted walnuts, pecans, or pepitas; sliced avocado for creaminess; or grilled corn for sweetness.

extras.label: Pairs with
extras.note: A bold red — Zinfandel or Syrah. Serve the warm steak over the cool salad for the temperature contrast.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
A house favorite that Stephen has dialed in over several rounds: the brown sugar in
the rub caramelizes against the cayenne for a sweet-hot crust, and blistering the
tomatoes on the same grill turns this from a plain steak salad into something with
real restaurant contrast. The blue cheese is the upgrade from the original feta.

recipes.title: Broccoli Rabe with Garlic & Chili
recipes.tagline: Ina Garten's quick skillet broccoli rabe with golden garlic and red pepper.
recipes.pitch: Broccoli rabe is all about balancing its pleasant bitterness, and this Barefoot Contessa classic nails it with almost nothing: slices of garlic cooked low in good olive oil until golden and sweet, pulled out, then put back at the end so they don't scorch. The rabe steams down in that same fragrant oil with a hit of crushed red pepper until the stalks are tender but still al dente. Five ingredients, ten minutes, and it tastes like a great Italian restaurant side. *Brown the garlic and lift it out before the greens go in — returning it at the end keeps it nutty instead of bitter.*

items: ¼ tsp crushed red pepper flakes
¼ tsp freshly ground black pepper
Rinse the broccoli rabe in a colander and drain well. Cut off and discard the tough ends, then cut the rest into 2-inch pieces.
Heat the olive oil in a large skillet and add the garlic. Cook over low heat, stirring occasionally, for 4 to 6 minutes, until golden brown. Remove the garlic with a slotted spoon and set aside.

Add the broccoli rabe to the hot oil along with the red pepper flakes, salt, and pepper. Cook, covered, over medium-to-low heat for 5 to 10 minutes, turning occasionally with tongs, until the stalks are tender but still al dente.
Add the reserved garlic back in, check the seasoning, and serve hot.

tips: To tame the bitterness further, blanch the rabe in boiling salted water for 1 minute and shock in ice water before it goes into the oil.
Don't rush the garlic — low and slow to golden builds the whole dish's flavor; pull it the moment it colors.
A squeeze of lemon or a few shavings of Parmesan at the end is a classic finish (skip the cheese to keep it vegan).

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Add a little fresh chopped jalapeño or serrano with the red pepper flakes for extra garden heat.

extras.label: Make it a meal
extras.note: Toss with cooked orecchiette or white beans and a splash of pasta water for a fast main.

source.name: Food Network — Ina Garten

recipes.headnote:
Ina Garten's no-fuss broccoli rabe: brown the garlic in good oil, lift it out, then
steam the greens in that fragrant oil with red pepper and return the garlic at the end.
Bitter, garlicky, and just spicy enough.

recipes.title: Broiled Fish with Citrus & Herb Sauce
recipes.tagline: A fast broiled fillet under a glossy reduced orange-tarragon sauce.
recipes.pitch: This is the cleanest kind of weeknight dinner: a mild fillet, oil-salt-pepper, and a ripping-hot broiler that turns it opaque and flaky in minutes. The quiet star is the sauce — orange juice simmered down with shallot and tarragon until it's syrupy and concentrated, spooned over the fish with a few chopped Kalamata olives for a salty bite. Naturally dairy- and gluten-free, and endlessly adaptable to whatever's in the crisper. *Reduce the juice until it coats a spoon — that slow simmer is what turns plain orange juice into a real pan sauce.*

items: 2 mild fish fillets (sole, tilapia, or arctic char), about 4 oz each
Combine the orange juice, shallot, and tarragon in a small saucepan. Simmer until thickened and syrupy, 15–20 minutes. Cover and set aside.
Heat the broiler. Rub both sides of the fish with the olive oil and season with the salt and pepper.
Place the fillets on a parchment-lined baking sheet and broil 5–6 inches from the heat until opaque and just flaking, about 5 minutes per half-inch of thickness.
Transfer each fillet to a plate with a wide spatula, spoon the citrus sauce over the top, and scatter with chopped olives.

tips: Watch the broiler like a hawk — thin fillets can go from perfect to dry in under a minute.
No orange juice? Reduce white wine or water with a good squeeze of lemon instead; swap dried dill for the tarragon.
For extra crunch and richness, finish with toasted nuts or seeds in place of (or alongside) the olives.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Fresh tarragon or thyme from the garden, stirred in at the end, lifts the sauce beyond the dried version.

extras.label: Pairs with
extras.note: Steamed rice or crushed potatoes and a green vegetable — the sauce ties it all together.

source.name: Whole Foods Market

recipes.headnote:
A no-fuss broiled fillet that earns its keep with a reduced orange-tarragon sauce.
Simmer the juice down until it's glossy, broil the fish fast, and dinner's on the
table in 25 minutes.

recipes.title: Broiled Sea Bass with Citrus, Olive & Caper Sauce
recipes.tagline: Fast-broiled sea bass under a bright raw sauce of lemon, Kalamata, and capers.
recipes.pitch: This is a restaurant trick disguised as a weeknight dinner: preheat the broiler pan screaming hot so the fish sears the instant it lands, then broil it just six minutes to silky, just-set flesh. While it cooks, you stir together a no-cook sauce of halved lemon segments, fresh oregano, briny capers, and chopped Kalamata olives slicked with olive oil. Spoon it over the hot fillets and the citrus juice and brine wake the whole plate up. *Heating the pan empty first is the secret — the fish gets a hard sear on contact, no flipping required.*

items: 2 sea bass fillets (about 5 oz each), skin on
¼ tsp freshly ground black pepper, divided
1 lemon, peeled and thinly sliced, segments halved
3 tbsp pitted Kalamata olives, roughly chopped
Position the broiler pan as close to the heating element as possible and heat it for 5 minutes.
Meanwhile, coat the fillets on both sides with about 1 tsp of the oil on a plate.
Carefully pull the hot pan out and set it on the stovetop. Arrange the fillets skin-side down and season with half the salt and pepper. Broil for 6 minutes.
In a bowl, combine the lemon slices, lemon juice, oregano, capers, olives, the remaining oil, and the remaining salt and pepper.
Transfer the fish to a platter and spoon the citrus-olive-caper sauce over the top.

tips: Black sea bass, branzino, or any firm white fish all work beautifully — keep the skin on so it crisps under the broiler.
Make the sauce first so the flavors meld while the pan heats and the fish cooks.
No need to flip — a properly preheated pan sears the underside while the broiler cooks the top through.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Use fresh oregano (or a mix of oregano and basil) straight from the garden, and add a pinch of sliced serrano to the sauce for heat.

extras.label: Pairs with
extras.note: Serve over couscous, white beans, or roasted potatoes to catch the bright, briny sauce.

source.name: Epicurious — Liza Schoenfein

recipes.headnote:
Liza Schoenfein's quick broiler method, scaled for two: preheat the pan blazing hot,
broil the fish six minutes, and finish with a raw sauce of lemon, oregano, capers, and
Kalamata. Bright, briny, and on the table in 25 minutes.

recipes.title: Brown Butter Banana Bread with Coconut & Chocolate
recipes.tagline: Nutty browned butter, dark chocolate chunks, and a tropical hit of coconut in one ridiculously good loaf.
recipes.pitch: Banana bread, but it went to finishing school. The butter gets browned until it smells like toffee, which threads a deep, nutty caramel through the whole loaf, while a spoonful of cream of coconut keeps the crumb impossibly moist and lends a faint tropical sweetness. Pools of chopped dark chocolate and toasted walnuts do the rest. It bakes up with a craggy, sugar-crisp top and a tender, almost custardy center. *Brown the butter past golden to genuinely nutty — those toasty bits are the whole point, so don't pull it early.*

items: 3 tbsp cream of coconut (such as Coco López)
¾ cup chopped dark chocolate or chunks, a little reserved for the top
½ cup walnuts, toasted and chopped

Brown the butter — melt it in a small saucepan over medium heat, swirling often, until it foams, smells nutty, and brown flecks form on the bottom, 5–7 minutes. Pour into a large bowl and let cool 5–10 minutes.
Toast the walnuts — spread them on a sheet pan and bake at 350°F for 7–9 minutes until fragrant and slightly darkened, then cool and chop.
Whisk the brown sugar and granulated sugar into the warm (not hot) browned butter, then whisk in the mashed bananas, eggs, cream of coconut, vanilla, and lime zest until smooth.
In a separate bowl, whisk the flour, baking soda, salt, cinnamon, and cardamom. Fold the dry into the wet just until combined — a few flour streaks are fine.
Fold in the chocolate, walnuts, and shredded coconut, saving a little chocolate for the top.
Scrape into a greased or parchment-lined 9×5-inch loaf pan, scatter the reserved chocolate over, and bake at 350°F for 50–60 minutes, until a toothpick comes out with moist crumbs (not wet batter).
Cool in the pan 15 minutes, then turn out onto a wire rack to cool completely before slicing.

tips: Bananas not ripe enough? Roast them whole in their skins at 300°F for 15 minutes until soft and blackened, then mash.
Don't overmix once the flour goes in — overworked batter bakes up tough and domed instead of tender.
For a crackly, bakery-style top, sprinkle a tablespoon of turbinado or raw sugar over the batter before it bakes.
Chopped bar chocolate melts into luscious pools; chips hold their shape if you'd rather have distinct bites.

extras.label: No cream of coconut?
extras.note: Swap in ¼ cup sour cream or Greek yogurt — less tropical, every bit as moist.

extras.label: Make it muffins
extras.note: Divide into a lined 12-cup tin and bake at 350°F for about 20–24 minutes.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
Brown butter is the cheat code for banana bread — five extra minutes at the stove for a
loaf that tastes like it came from a very good bakery.

recipes.title: Brown Butter Sugar Cookies
recipes.tagline: Crackly-topped sugar cookies with a deep, toffee-nutty brown butter backbone.
recipes.pitch: A sugar cookie has no business tasting this complex. The trick is browning the butter until it turns the color of maple and smells like toasted hazelnuts, then chilling it back to a scoopable solid so it can be creamed like normal. That nutty, caramelized fat gives the cookies a chewy center and a sugar-crusted, crackled top that shatters on the first bite. *Chilling the browned butter is non-negotiable — solid butter creams and traps air; melted butter just makes a greasy puddle.*

items: 1¼ cups granulated sugar, plus more for rolling
Brown the butter — melt it in a saucepan over medium heat, swirling, until it foams and smells nutty with brown flecks on the bottom, 5–7 minutes.
Pour the browned butter into a bowl and chill until semi-solid and scoopable, about 30–45 minutes in the fridge.
Cream the chilled browned butter with the 1¼ cups sugar until pale and fluffy, then beat in the egg, yolk, and vanilla.
In a separate bowl, whisk the flour, baking soda, baking powder, and salt, then mix into the butter just until combined.
Roll the dough into balls, roll each in granulated sugar, and space them on parchment-lined sheets.
Bake at 350°F for 10–12 minutes, until set at the edges but still soft in the center.
Let the cookies rest on the sheet a few minutes to finish setting before moving them to a rack.

tips: Don't skip chilling the browned butter — it has to be semi-solid to cream properly, or the cookies spread into thin, greasy disks.
Roll the dough balls generously in sugar for that signature crackly, sparkling top.
Pull them while the centers still look slightly underdone — they firm up on the hot sheet and stay chewy.
For evenly sized cookies, scoop the dough with a tablespoon measure or small disher before rolling.

extras.label: Lemon sugar cookies
extras.note: Rub 1 tbsp lemon zest into the sugar before creaming for a bright, citrusy version.

extras.label: Make it ahead
extras.note: Freeze the sugared dough balls on a tray, then bake straight from frozen — just add a minute or two.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
Browning the butter is five minutes of work for a sugar cookie that tastes like it has a
secret — nutty, deep, and impossible to eat just one of.

recipes.title: Brown Sugar Sweet Potato Casserole
recipes.tagline: Silky brown-sugar sweet potatoes under a crunchy pecan-streusel crown.
recipes.pitch: This is the holiday side Lauren remembers — creamy, cinnamon-warm sweet potatoes that taste almost like pie, finished with a buttery brown-sugar pecan streusel that bakes into a craggy, golden crust. The base gets mashed smooth with cream and vanilla, then the topping caramelizes on top until the edges go crisp and the sugar bubbles. *The trick is the textural one-two: velvet underneath, candied crunch on top, in every single spoonful.* It comes together in one dish and holds beautifully on a crowded Thanksgiving table.

items: 4–5 medium sweet potatoes (about 3 lbs), peeled and cubed
¼ cup heavy cream (or a dairy-free alternative)
Heat the oven to 375°F and butter a 9x13 baking dish.
Boil the cubed sweet potatoes in salted water until fork-tender, about 20 minutes. Drain well and let the steam dissipate for a minute so the mash isn't watery.
Mash the sweet potatoes until smooth, then beat in the melted butter, brown sugar, cream, vanilla, cinnamon, nutmeg, and salt. Taste and adjust the sweetness.
Spread the mixture into the prepared dish in an even layer.
In a bowl, rub the pecans, brown sugar, melted butter, flour, and salt together with your fingers until crumbly. Scatter evenly over the sweet potatoes.
Bake 25–30 minutes, until the topping is golden and the edges are bubbling. Let rest 5–10 minutes before serving so it sets.

tips: Roasting the sweet potatoes instead of boiling concentrates their sugars — halve them, roast cut-side down at 400°F until collapsing, then scoop and mash for a deeper flavor.
Toast the pecans in a dry skillet for a few minutes before mixing the streusel; it doubles their aroma in the finished crust.
Assemble the whole casserole a day ahead and refrigerate unbaked; add 10 minutes to the bake time straight from the fridge.
If you like a crisper top, run it under the broiler for the last 1–2 minutes — watch closely so the sugar doesn't scorch.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Stir a pinch of chopped thyme or a few torn sage leaves into the streusel for a savory edge that cuts the sweetness.

extras.label: Make it dairy-free
extras.note: Swap the butter for a plant-based stick and use full-fat coconut cream in the base — the vanilla and cinnamon carry the flavor regardless.

extras.label: Pairs with
extras.note: A natural alongside glazed rainbow carrots and roasted fingerlings; rounds out a turkey-free holiday spread.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
The sweet potato side Lauren remembered from past holidays, dialed in for Stephen's
turkey-free Thanksgiving: a smooth, vanilla-scented base under a brown-sugar pecan
streusel that bakes into a candied, crunchy crown.

recipes.title: Chicken Piccata with Thin Spaghetti & Lemon-Caper Pan Sauce
recipes.tagline: Golden chicken cutlets in a buttery lemon-caper sauce, tangled with thin spaghetti.
recipes.pitch: This is the piccata that earns its butter. Thin chicken cutlets get a quick flour dredge and a hard sear until the edges go lacquer-brown, then the pan becomes the whole show: white wine deglazed against the fond, lemon, briny capers, and cold butter swirled in off the heat until the sauce turns glossy and clings. Thin spaghetti drinks it all up, and crispy fried capers scattered on top give you little salty pops of crunch. *The trick is to swirl the butter into the sauce off the heat — that's how you get a silky emulsion instead of a greasy puddle.*

items: 2 boneless skinless chicken breasts, sliced horizontally into 4 thin cutlets
½ cup all-purpose flour (whole wheat works too)
Kosher salt and freshly ground black pepper
Juice of 1 lemon, plus zest of ½ lemon
2 tbsp capers, drained (plus 1 tbsp extra for crisping)
2 tbsp fresh parsley, chopped (from the garden)
Pound the cutlets gently to an even ¼-inch thickness. Season the flour with salt and pepper, then dredge the cutlets and shake off the excess.
Bring a pot of salted water to a boil for the spaghetti.
Heat the olive oil and 1 tbsp butter in a large skillet over medium-high. Add the extra 1 tbsp capers and fry until they burst open and crisp, about 1 minute; lift out and drain on a paper towel.
Add the chicken to the same pan and cook 3 minutes per side until deep golden and cooked through. Remove and tent loosely with foil.
Lower the heat to medium. Add the garlic and chili flakes and sauté 30 seconds until fragrant. Pour in the white wine and let it reduce by half, scraping up the browned bits.
Add the broth, lemon juice, lemon zest, and the 2 tbsp capers. Simmer 3–4 minutes until reduced slightly.
Pull the pan off the heat and swirl in the cold butter a few cubes at a time until the sauce is glossy and emulsified. Taste and adjust salt and lemon.
Meanwhile, cook the spaghetti until just al dente, reserving a splash of pasta water. Drain and toss the pasta directly into the sauce, loosening with pasta water if needed.
Return the chicken to the pan, spooning sauce over the top to rewarm for a minute.
Plate the spaghetti, lay the cutlets on top, and finish with parsley, crispy capers, lemon slices, and pine nuts if using.

tips: Pounding the cutlets to an even thickness is the whole game — they cook in minutes and stay juicy instead of drying out at the thin ends.
Swirl the butter in off the heat; too much direct heat breaks the emulsion and you lose the glossy, restaurant-style finish.
A heaping spoonful of reserved pasta water helps the sauce coat the spaghetti instead of sliding off it.
Fry the capers first in the chicken oil — they turn into salty little crisps that are the best garnish on the plate.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Finish with a generous handful of garden parsley, and wilt a few thinly sliced garden greens or spinach into the sauce at the end for color.

extras.label: Make it a meal
extras.note: Serve with garlic-sautéed broccoli — sear the florets hard, then add garlic, a pinch of chili flakes, and a squeeze of lemon.

extras.label: Pairs with
extras.note: A crisp, unoaked white — Pinot Grigio or Sauvignon Blanc — to echo the lemon.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
The piccata Stephen has refined across a few rounds: a wine-and-lemon pan sauce
finished with cold butter for gloss, plus crispy fried capers on top for crunch.
Thin spaghetti is the perfect tangle to carry the bright, briny sauce.

recipes.title: Chunky Mango-Habanero Salsa
recipes.tagline: Sweet-heat tropical salsa — ripe mango, cool cucumber, and a slow habanero burn.
recipes.pitch: Sweet and dangerous in the same bite: ripe mango and cool cucumber up front, then a creeping habanero heat that builds a beat later. Everything is diced small and tossed raw — no blender, no cooking — so it stays bright, crunchy, and chunky, somewhere between a salsa and a relish. *Start the habanero smaller than you think you need; you can always add fire, but you can't take it back.* Lime zest and juice keep it electric, and a chiffonade of garden basil gives it an unexpected, almost-caprese lift. It's made for grilled fish or spooned straight into an avocado half.

items: 1 ripe mango, peeled and diced small
¼ cup red cherry tomatoes, quartered
¼ cup green cherry tomatoes, halved or quartered
1 small cucumber, peeled, seeded, and diced
1 jalapeño, finely diced (seeded for less heat)
1 serrano, very finely diced (with seeds if you're bold)
½ habanero, minced very finely (start with less if unsure)
2 tbsp fresh basil, chiffonade (or cilantro for a classic profile)
Pinch of kosher salt, to taste
Dice the mango, cucumber, and onion small and keep everything roughly uniform — aim for a salsa-crudo texture, never pureed.
Mince the jalapeño, serrano, and habanero very finely. Taste a sliver of each first; heat varies, so adjust to your comfort.
In a bowl, combine the mango, both tomatoes, cucumber, onion, all the peppers, and garlic.
Add the lime zest and juice, the vinegar if using, salt, and basil. Stir gently to combine.
Let it sit at room temperature 10–15 minutes (or chill up to 4 hours) so the flavors meld. Taste and adjust salt, lime, and heat before serving.

tips: Balance the heat by adding more mango or cucumber, not by skimping on flavor — sweetness and cool crunch tame the habanero.
Treat the basil like mint — let it lift the salsa rather than dominate. A tiny chiffonade goes a long way.
For a smoky depth, grill the mango (or cucumber) briefly before dicing — the char plays beautifully against the fruit's sweetness.
No fresh mango? Stir in 2–3 tbsp pineapple juice, or simmer ¼ cup down to a syrupy reduction and use a spoonful for concentrated tropical punch.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: This is a showcase for garden serrano, jalapeño, habanero, cherry tomatoes, and basil. Use both red and green tomatoes for color and a tart-sweet contrast.

extras.label: Serving ideas
extras.note: Spoon over grilled or seared fish, black-bean tacos, or seared tofu; pile into avocado halves; or scoop with salty tortilla or plantain chips.

extras.label: Make it umami
extras.note: A tiny splash of soy sauce or fish sauce adds savory intrigue under the sweetness — just a few drops.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
A merger of Stephen's two tropical salsa experiments: the sweet-heat mango-cucumber
version and the chunky garden-fire one, brought together with a slow habanero burn and
a bright basil lift. Raw, crunchy, and built to sit on grilled fish.

recipes.title: Cioppino — Build-Your-Own Seafood Stew
recipes.tagline: A garlicky saffron-fennel-tomato broth loaded with white fish and your pick of shellfish.
recipes.pitch: A true batch recipe and a stunner for company: a garlicky, saffron-and-fennel tomato broth — basically the poached-cod-in-saffron-broth already in this book, scaled into a feast — built so anyone at the table loads it with exactly what they like. *You build the broth, then the seafood poaches in it in eight minutes, perfuming the whole pot.* Firm white fish is the constant (and exactly what Stephen eats); clams, mussels, crab, and calamari are all swap-in add-ins. Serve with grilled bread to mop the bowl.

ingredients.section: The seafood (white fish is the constant — add any mix of the rest)
items: 1 lb cod or halibut, cut into 2-inch chunks
½ lb mussels, scrubbed and debearded
¼ lb lump crab or calamari

Heat the oil in a large Dutch oven over medium. Cook the onion and fennel until soft, 6–8 minutes. Add the garlic and red pepper flakes and cook 1 minute, then stir in the tomato paste and cook 1 minute more.
Add the wine and simmer 2 minutes. Stir in the crushed tomatoes, fish stock, saffron, bay leaf, and oregano. Simmer 20 minutes, until the broth is rich and fragrant. Season with salt.
Tuck the clams and mussels into the broth, cover, and cook 3–4 minutes, until they begin to open.
Add the fish chunks (and crab or calamari) and simmer gently 4–5 minutes, until the fish is just opaque and the shells have all opened. Discard any that stay shut.
Shower with parsley and ladle into bowls. Serve with grilled bread and lemon wedges.

tips: The fish and broth are the whole recipe — the clams, mussels, crab, and calamari are all optional. Use any mix to about 1 lb total, or skip them entirely for a fish-only stew that Stephen can dig into.
Poach gently and pull the pot the moment the fish turns opaque; seafood overcooks fast in hot broth.
The broth is even better made a day ahead — cool and chill it, then reheat and add the seafood just before serving.
No fish stock? Use bottled clam juice, or a mix of vegetable broth and a splash of the wine.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Fennel fronds and parsley over the top; a little thyme or basil in the broth.

extras.label: Make it a feast
extras.note: Double the broth and pile in a true mix — clams, mussels, crab, calamari, and two kinds of fish — for a crowd.

extras.label: Pairs with
extras.note: Lots of grilled, garlic-rubbed bread and a crisp white or rosé.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
San Francisco's great Italian-American fishermen's stew — built to use whatever came off
the boat. Here it's a choose-your-own pot: one broth, and everyone's bowl their own way.

recipes.title: Citrus Salmon with Blistered Asparagus
recipes.tagline: Bright, weeknight-easy salmon and asparagus with a glossy citrus finish.
recipes.pitch: This is the salmon you reach for on a Tuesday and still feel proud serving on a Friday. Fillets get a quick citrus-garlic bath, then go skin-side down until the edges turn lacquered and the flesh just flakes, while asparagus blisters alongside in the same heat. A squeeze of lemon (or orange) at the end pulls everything into focus — sweet, savory, herby, clean. *The whole point: one bright pan, two ways, and dinner on the table in under half an hour.*

items: 1 tbsp fresh dill or parsley, chopped
Salt and freshly ground black pepper, to taste
Salt and freshly ground black pepper, to taste
Pat the salmon completely dry. In a small bowl, whisk the olive oil, minced garlic, lemon juice, lemon zest, and chopped herbs with a pinch of salt and pepper.
Brush the citrus-garlic mixture over the fillets and let them sit at room temperature 10–15 minutes while you trim the asparagus and toss it with oil, garlic, salt, and pepper.

Heat 2 tbsp olive oil in a cast-iron skillet over medium-high. Lay the salmon skin-side down and press gently for 15 seconds so it stays flat. Cook 4–5 minutes until the skin is crisp and the fish is 70% opaque, then flip and finish 1–2 minutes.
Move the salmon to a plate. In the same skillet, sauté the asparagus 5–7 minutes until tender-crisp and lightly blistered. Spoon any pan juices and a squeeze of lemon over both.
Return the salmon to the pan for a final minute, spooning the citrus pan sauce over the top. The fish is done when it flakes easily and reads 125–130°F at the thickest point.
Plate the salmon and asparagus, shower with Parmesan if using, and finish with lemon slices, fresh herbs, and a wedge on the side.

tips: Dry skin is crisp skin — blot the fillets twice and don't crowd the pan, or they steam instead of sear.
Pull the salmon at 125°F; carryover heat finishes it to a silky 130°F while it rests, so it never dries out.
Start the salmon first and add the asparagus once you flip the fish, so both land hot at the same moment.
A tight-lidded jar mellows raw garlic in the marinade — give it a 10-minute head start before brushing it on.

extras.label: Lemon-garlic, pan or bake
extras.note: To bake instead, set the oven to 400°F, brush the fillets with the citrus-garlic mix, roast salmon and asparagus on one sheet 12–15 minutes, then broil 2 minutes for a golden top.

extras.label: Orange-glazed on the grill
extras.note: Swap lemon for an orange marinade and fire up the grill to medium-high (375–400°F). Grill skin-on fillets skin-side down 4–5 minutes, flip 3–4 minutes to 145°F; add oiled asparagus after 2–3 minutes so both finish together. Brush with reserved (uncontaminated) marinade for shine.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Stir chopped garden dill or basil into the marinade, and finish with a little fresh thyme. Garden lemon or a Meyer lemon makes the brightest glaze.

extras.label: Pairs with
extras.note: A crisp Sauvignon Blanc or unoaked Chardonnay. Round it out with buttery mashed potatoes, wild rice, or a simple garden salad.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
The salmon that earns a permanent spot in the weeknight rotation: a fast
citrus-garlic marinade, a crisp skin from the cast iron, and asparagus blistered
in the very same pan. Lemon keeps it bright on the stove or in the oven; swap in
orange and the grill for a sweeter, smokier summer version.

recipes.title: Classic Pumpkin Pie from Fresh Pumpkin
recipes.tagline: A from-scratch roasted-pumpkin custard in a buttery, flaky crust — the real Thanksgiving thing.
recipes.pitch: This is the pumpkin pie that ruins canned filling for you forever. You roast a sugar pumpkin until it slumps and sweetens, purée it down, and whisk it into a glossy spiced custard set with eggs and evaporated milk. It pours into an all-butter crust and bakes into a deep, burnished round that quivers ever so slightly in the center when you pull it. *Roast your own pumpkin — the purée is richer, less watery, and tastes like actual squash instead of a tin.*

items: ½ cup cold unsalted butter, cubed
3–4 tbsp ice water (or use a good store-bought crust)
1 cup evaporated milk or heavy cream

Halve and seed the pumpkin, rub the cut sides with the oil, and roast cut-side down at 400°F for 35–45 minutes until fork-tender. Scoop and purée smooth, draining in a sieve if watery, then measure out 2 cups.
For the crust, mix the flour, salt, and sugar, then cut in the cold butter until the mixture looks like coarse crumbs.
Add ice water a tablespoon at a time, just until the dough holds together, then chill it 30 minutes.
Roll the dough out and fit it into a 9-inch pie dish, crimp the edge, and chill again while you make the filling.
For the filling, whisk the eggs and yolk, then whisk in the purée, both sugars, salt, spices, and vanilla.
Stream in the evaporated milk or cream and whisk until completely smooth, then pour into the chilled crust.
Bake at 425°F for 15 minutes, then reduce the heat to 350°F and bake 40–45 minutes more, until the center is just set and barely jiggles.
Cool completely, about 2 hours, before slicing so the custard firms up.

tips: A fresh pie pumpkin beats canned every time — the purée is richer, sweeter, and far less watery.
If your purée seems loose, drain it in a sieve or cheesecloth before measuring, or the custard won't set cleanly.
Resist cutting it warm — let the pie cool fully so the custard sets into clean, sliceable wedges.
Serve with lightly sweetened whipped cream or a thin drizzle of maple syrup over each slice.

extras.label: Make it ahead
extras.note: Bake the pie a day early and refrigerate — the flavor deepens overnight and the slices cut even cleaner cold.

extras.label: Blind-bake insurance
extras.note: For an extra-crisp bottom, line the crust with foil and pie weights and bake 12 minutes at 375°F before filling.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
The whole pie hinges on the purée — roast a real sugar pumpkin and you'll taste the
difference in every forkful.

recipes.title: Coconut Chicken Soup (Tom Kha Gai)
recipes.tagline: A silky coconut-lime broth with poached chicken, mushrooms, and a hit of fresh chile.
recipes.pitch: This is the bowl you want when you need comfort with a pulse. A fragrant base of ginger, lemongrass, and garlic gets loosened with coconut milk into a broth that's rich but not heavy, then balanced on the knife's edge of salty fish sauce and sharp lime. Poached chicken and mushrooms slip in, and the whole thing finishes with torn cilantro and a few thin coins of fresh chile to clear your head. *Add the lime juice off the heat at the very end — boiling it turns the brightness flat and bitter.*

items: 1 thumb fresh ginger, sliced into coins
1 stalk lemongrass, bruised and cut into 2-inch lengths (or extra ginger)
1–2 tsp Thai chili paste (or 1 fresh chile, sliced)
2 boneless skinless chicken breasts, thinly sliced
1 serrano or Thai chile, thinly sliced (from the garden)
Heat the oil in a saucepan over medium. Add the ginger, lemongrass, and garlic and stir 1–2 minutes until fragrant.
Pour in the chicken broth and coconut milk, stir in the chili paste and sugar, and bring to a gentle simmer (don't let it hard-boil, which can split the coconut milk).
Add the sliced chicken and mushrooms. Simmer gently 8–10 minutes until the chicken is just cooked through and the broth has taken on the aromatics.
Fish out the lemongrass and large ginger coins if you like. Stir in fish sauce to taste.
Take the pot off the heat. Stir in the lime juice, tasting as you go — you want it bright and balanced between salty, sour, and rich.
Ladle into bowls (over rice if using) and top with torn cilantro and thin slices of fresh chile.

tips: Keep the broth at a bare simmer, never a rolling boil — high heat splits coconut milk into oily and grainy parts.
Balance at the end on the trio of fish sauce (salt), lime (sour), and a pinch of sugar (sweet). Adjust one at a time until the broth makes you want another spoonful.
Slice the chicken thin and add it raw to the simmering broth; it cooks in minutes and stays tender instead of rubbery.
No lemongrass? A wider strip of lime zest plus extra ginger gets you most of the way to that citrusy aroma.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: A few thin coins of garden serrano or Thai chile floated on top bring gentle sinus-clearing heat, and a heavy handful of cilantro is essential.

extras.label: Make it heartier
extras.note: Serve over jasmine rice, or add a handful of rice noodles to the pot in the last 3 minutes.

extras.label: Pairs with
extras.note: Crusty bread for dunking, or just a wedge of extra lime on the side.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
The cozy bowl Stephen reaches for under the weather: warm, soothing, and just
spicy enough to clear the sinuses. Coconut milk for richness, lime and fish
sauce for brightness, and fresh chile to wake it all up.

recipes.title: Creamy Jalapeño-Cilantro Salsa (Inspired by Fresa's)
recipes.tagline: A smooth, dairy-free green salsa with a serious kick — charred peppers blended creamy with cilantro and lime.
recipes.pitch: This is the addictive green sauce from Fresa's, the one with the deceptive creaminess and a back-of-the-throat heat — and it has no dairy in it at all. Charred jalapeños and serranos get blended with cilantro, garlic, and lime, then turned silky by slowly streaming in oil until it emulsifies into a pale, pourable green. *The magic is that emulsion: a thin drizzle of oil while the blender runs is what makes it taste like crema with zero cream.* Roasting the peppers first rounds the raw bite into something smoky and deep. Pour it over tacos, eggs, or anything that needs waking up.

items: 6 fresh jalapeños, stemmed (seed some for less heat)
2 serrano peppers, stemmed (optional, for extra heat)
1 cup fresh cilantro leaves and tender stems
½ tsp kosher salt, plus more to taste
½ cup neutral oil (canola or grapeseed)
Set the broiler to high and position a rack 4–6 inches below the element. Line a sheet pan with foil.

Spread the jalapeños, serranos, and unpeeled garlic on the pan. Broil about 10 minutes, rotating the peppers every 2–3 minutes, until blistered and charred all over. Tuck the garlic under a pepper so it softens gently without burning.
Let everything cool slightly. Peel the garlic. Seed some or all of the peppers if you want to dial the heat back.
In a blender, combine the roasted peppers, garlic, cilantro, lime juice, and salt. Blend until mostly smooth.
With the blender running on low, slowly drizzle in the oil until the salsa turns creamy, pale green, and emulsified. Thin with a tablespoon of water at a time if needed.
Taste and adjust salt and lime. Let it rest at least 30 minutes for the flavors to meld before serving.

tips: The slow oil drizzle is non-negotiable — pour it in a thin stream while blending so it emulsifies instead of separating.
Keep the garlic slightly off to the side under the broiler, or tuck it beneath a pepper; it needs a gentler roast than the peppers.
Taste a sliver of each pepper before blending — heat varies wildly even within one batch, so seed accordingly.
The salsa deepens overnight; if it's too fiery fresh, a night in the fridge mellows the edge.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Built for garden jalapeños and serranos. For a smokier, slightly sweet version, char a handful of ripe cherry tomatoes alongside the peppers and blend them in.

extras.label: No-cook shortcut
extras.note: Skip the broiler and blend the peppers raw for a brighter, grassier, more aggressive heat — faster, with a more vivid green.

extras.label: Storage
extras.note: Keeps in an airtight container in the fridge for up to a week. Great on tacos, grilled meats, eggs, or avocado toast.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
Stephen's take on the creamy green salsa from Fresa's: charred jalapeños and serranos
blended with cilantro and lime, then emulsified silky-smooth with a slow stream of oil —
all the richness of crema, none of the dairy.

recipes.title: Crispy Broiled Salmon with Charred Broccoli & Lemon-Herb Brown Butter
recipes.tagline: Shatter-crisp broiled salmon skin over charred broccoli and blistered tomatoes, with a lemon-caper brown butter.
recipes.pitch: The whole game here is the skin. Pat the fillets bone-dry, score them, slick them with oil, and slide them skin-side-up close under a roaring broiler until the skin blisters into something that crackles under a fork while the flesh stays just-set and buttery. Broccoli roasts hot in the same oven until the tips char and go nutty, and garden cherry tomatoes burst sweet-tart against it. *A lemon-caper brown butter — nutty, briny, sharp with Dijon — ties cold acid to hot fat and pulls the whole plate together.* It's a weeknight dinner that eats like a bistro special.

items: Kosher salt and freshly ground black pepper
Neutral oil (grapeseed or avocado), for the skin
1 small head broccoli (~3–4 cups florets), cut into bite-sized pieces
Kosher salt and freshly ground black pepper
2 sprigs fresh thyme (or ¼ tsp dried)
Pinch of chili flakes or Aleppo pepper (optional)
Move an oven rack to about 6 inches below the broiler. You'll roast the vegetables at 450°F first, then switch to broil for the salmon.
Pat the salmon very dry, especially the skin. Score the skin 2–3 times with a sharp knife (don't cut into the flesh), season both sides, and rub the skin with a little neutral oil.
Toss the broccoli and sliced garlic with the olive oil, lemon zest, salt, and pepper on a sheet pan. Roast at 450°F for 10 minutes.
Add the halved cherry tomatoes (tossed with a pinch of salt) and roast 6–8 minutes more, until the broccoli is charred at the tips and the tomatoes have softened but still hold. Don't crowd the pan.
Switch the oven to broil on high. Place the salmon skin-side up on an oiled, foil-lined sheet pan.

Broil 6–8 minutes, until the skin is deeply golden and shatter-crisp and the flesh just flakes. Rotate the pan halfway if your broiler runs uneven; for thick fillets, finish with a 1–2 minute rest in the turned-off oven.
Melt the butter in a small saucepan over medium heat, swirling, until it foams then browns to amber with a nutty aroma, about 3–5 minutes. Pull it off the heat.
Stir in the Dijon, capers, thyme, lemon zest, and optional chili. Taste and brighten with a few drops of lemon juice.
Toss the roasted vegetables with a squeeze of lemon and divide between plates. Nestle the salmon on top, skin-side up, and spoon the warm brown butter over the fish and the vegetables.

tips: Dry skin plus high heat plus oil equals maximum crisp; scoring the skin keeps the fillet from curling and lifting off the pan.
Broiling skin-side up renders the fat and crackles the skin while shielding the flesh from the direct element, so it stays moist.
Pour the brown butter into a warm bowl the moment it's amber — leftover pan heat will tip it from nutty to burnt in seconds.
Salmon carries over off the heat, so pull it a touch early; the residual heat finishes it while you make the sauce.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Garden cherry tomatoes are the move here — they burst sweeter than store-bought. Finish with torn basil or parsley from the bed.

extras.label: Make it a full plate
extras.note: Spoon it all over a quick herbed couscous, farro, or quinoa to soak up the brown butter.

extras.label: Pairs with
extras.note: A chilled Sauvignon Blanc or Grüner Veltliner, or a citrusy wheat beer to match the lemon.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
Stephen's weeknight-elevated salmon: the broiler does the work of a restaurant
salamander, crackling the skin in minutes, while the broccoli chars in the same
oven and garden tomatoes burst alongside. The lemon-caper brown butter is the
upgrade that turns a simple fish-and-vegetable plate into something worth setting
the table for.

recipes.title: Crispy Oven Kale Chips
recipes.tagline: Shatter-crisp, salty kale leaves baked low and slow into a snackable chip.
recipes.pitch: The secret to kale chips that crackle instead of wilt is restraint — dry leaves, the lightest film of oil, and a low oven that dehydrates rather than steams. Torn into bite-size pieces and spread in a single uncrowded layer, the kale shrinks and curls until the edges go translucent and shatter at a touch. *Bone-dry leaves and barely-there oil are everything: a wet or over-oiled chip steams limp instead of crisping.* A pinch of smoked paprika or garlic powder turns a healthy habit into something you'll fight over.

items: ½ tsp kosher salt, or to taste
Optional seasonings: garlic powder, smoked paprika, or chili flakes
Heat the oven to 300°F and line two baking sheets with parchment.
Wash the kale and dry it thoroughly — a salad spinner plus a few minutes on a towel. Any clinging water steams the leaves instead of crisping them.
Strip the leaves from the tough stems and tear them into bite-size pieces.
Toss the kale with just enough olive oil to lightly coat — the leaves should look barely glossy, not slick. Massage it in with your hands.
Spread in a single, uncrowded layer across both sheets. Sprinkle with salt and any optional seasonings.
Bake 20–25 minutes, rotating the pans and flipping the leaves halfway, until the edges are crisp but not browned.
Cool a few minutes on the sheet; they firm up as they cool. Eat the same day for peak crunch.

tips: Less oil is more — over-oiled chips go limp and greasy. A teaspoon per packed handful is plenty.
Season after baking if using fine salt or grated cheese-style toppings so they don't slide off the wet leaves.
If a few chips still feel bendy, return them to the warm (turned-off) oven for 5 minutes to finish drying.
Store fully cooled chips with a paper towel in an airtight container; they keep their crunch for a day or two.

extras.label: Dehydrator method
extras.note: You own a dehydrator — run the oiled, seasoned leaves at 125°F for 4–6 hours for the crispest, most evenly dried chips with zero risk of scorching.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Finish with a dusting of garden chili flakes, or toss in a little minced serrano with the oil for a slow-building heat.

extras.label: Flavor swaps
extras.note: Try nutritional yeast and garlic powder for a cheesy vibe, or a pinch of cumin and lime zest for a taco-night snack.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
A reliable, low-and-slow method for kale chips that actually stay crisp — the whole
game is dry leaves and a barely-there coat of oil.

recipes.title: Crispy-Skin Sea Bass with Tomato-Jalapeño Confit & Herbed Cucumber Salad
recipes.tagline: Shatteringly crisp sea bass over warm, sweet-spicy tomato confit.
recipes.pitch: Crackling skin is the headline here, and it comes from one rule: dry fish, hot pan, don't touch. The fillets sear skin-side down until the surface shatters like glass, set over a low, slow confit of cherry tomatoes, garlic, and jalapeño that turns jammy and sweet with a slow-building heat. A cool shaved-cucumber salad with rosemary, basil, and lemon zest snaps the whole plate awake. *Press the skin flat for the first fifteen seconds and let it ride — it releases on its own when it's truly crisp.*

items: 2 skin-on sea bass fillets (about 6 oz each), boneless
Salt and freshly ground black pepper
1 pint cherry or grape tomatoes, halved
1 jalapeño, minced (start with ½ if heat-sensitive)
1 tbsp white wine or champagne vinegar
Salt and cracked pepper, to taste
Optional: thinly sliced shallot or a few raw tomato halves
Flaky salt (Maldon or fleur de sel)
Pat the sea bass completely dry, especially the skin. Set the fillets skin-side up, uncovered, in the fridge while you make everything else — extra drying time means crispier skin.
In a small skillet or saucepan, combine the olive oil, halved tomatoes, minced jalapeño, garlic, thyme, and a pinch of salt.
Heat on low, swirling occasionally, until the tomatoes soften, blister, and turn slightly jammy, about 20–25 minutes. Keep it gentle — low and slow protects the confit's sweetness.
Toss the sliced cucumber with the rosemary, basil, lemon zest, vinegar, olive oil, salt, and pepper (and shallot or raw tomato if using). Don't overdress — let the herbs lead. Chill until plating.

Heat a cast-iron or stainless skillet over medium-high with 1–2 tbsp olive oil. Season the fillets with salt and pepper. When the oil shimmers, lay them skin-side down and press firmly with a spatula for 15 seconds to stop them curling.

Sear skin-side down 4–5 minutes until the fish is about 70% cooked through and the skin is golden and crisp. Flip and finish flesh-side down just 1–2 minutes. Don't move it while it sears — it releases naturally when ready.
Spoon a generous puddle of warm tomato-jalapeño confit onto each plate. Set the fish skin-side up over the confit so the skin stays crisp.
Add a mound of chilled cucumber salad and finish with lemon zest, flaky salt, and a sprig of basil.

tips: Skin-side up in the fridge, uncovered, for even 20 minutes wicks away surface moisture — the single biggest lever for crisp skin.
Press the fillets flat for the first 15 seconds only; after that, leave them be, and they'll lift cleanly when the skin is set.
Taste a sliver of your jalapeño first — heat varies wildly, so add it in stages and let the confit tell you when it's enough.
Serve immediately so the crisp skin doesn't steam against the warm confit; plate skin-up, never skin-down.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: This dish was built for the garden — use your own cherry tomatoes, jalapeños, rosemary, basil, and thyme. Sub a red serrano for the jalapeño for extra heat and a pop of color.

extras.label: Other firm fish
extras.note: Halibut, mahi mahi, or drum fillets all work; for skinless fillets, sear the presentation side hard and finish gently rather than chasing crisp skin.

extras.label: Pairs with
extras.note: A crisp, unoaked white — Vermentino, Sauvignon Blanc, or a dry rosé — keeps pace with the bright confit and herbs.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
A restaurant-style plate with very little fuss: a low, slow tomato-jalapeño confit
does its thing hands-off while you chill a herby cucumber salad, then the fish gets
its moment in a screaming pan for that signature crackling skin. Garden tomatoes
and peppers make it sing.

recipes.title: Filipino Chicken Adobo
recipes.tagline: Chicken thighs braised in soy, vinegar, garlic, and bay until glossy and falling apart.
recipes.pitch: The easiest deeply-savory braise you'll ever make, and a whole new cuisine in one pot. Chicken thighs simmer in soy, cane vinegar, a smashed head of garlic, bay, and black pepper until they're glossy and tender, the sauce reducing to a tangy-salty-sweet glaze you spoon over rice. *Vinegar and soy is the whole secret — it tastes like it took all day and asks for five minutes of work.* One pot, big leftovers, and it's even better the next night.

items: ⅓ cup cane vinegar (or apple cider vinegar)
Combine the chicken, soy, vinegar, garlic, bay, peppercorns, brown sugar, and water in a Dutch oven. If you have time, let it marinate 30 minutes.
Bring to a boil, then lower to a simmer, cover, and cook 30 minutes. Uncover and simmer 10–15 minutes more, until the sauce reduces and the chicken is tender.
For crispy edges, lift out the thighs and sear them skin-side down in the oil in a hot pan until browned, then reduce the sauce until glossy and return the chicken to coat.
Serve over jasmine rice with sliced scallions and plenty of the sauce.

tips: Don't stir while the vinegar first cooks — let it boil a couple of minutes so it mellows from sharp to savory before you stir.
For crispy skin, pull the thighs after braising and crisp them in a hot pan, then reduce the sauce to a glaze.
It genuinely improves overnight as the garlic and soy deepen — a great make-ahead.
A whole smashed head of garlic is correct here; it melts right into the sauce.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: A shower of garden scallion to finish, and a fresh bay leaf or two if you grow it.

extras.label: Make it a meal
extras.note: Steamed jasmine rice is non-negotiable for soaking up the salty-sour glaze.

extras.label: Swap
extras.note: Works just as well with drumsticks or a mix of bone-in chicken pieces.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
The unofficial national dish of the Philippines — and the rare braise that's genuinely
hard to mess up. Soy, vinegar, garlic, bay: four things that turn into something far
greater than their sum.

recipes.title: Fresh Pumpkin Bread (Spiced & Nutty)
recipes.tagline: Roasted-from-scratch pumpkin, a warm spice cabinet, and toasted nuts in one deeply moist loaf.
recipes.pitch: Canned pumpkin can't touch this. You roast a sugar pumpkin until it caramelizes at the edges, then purée it into something silkier and far more flavorful than anything from a tin. That fresh purée carries a whole spice rack — cinnamon, nutmeg, ginger, clove, a whisper of cardamom — and bakes into a dark, tender crumb shot through with toasted nuts and a sugar-crisp top. *Drain the purée until it's thick as canned — watery pumpkin is the only thing standing between you and a perfect crumb.*

items: ½ cup avocado or light olive oil
½ cup chopped toasted nuts (walnuts, almonds, or hazelnuts)
Halve and seed the pumpkin, then roast it cut-side down at 400°F for 35–45 minutes until fork-tender. Scoop the flesh from the skin and purée until smooth.
If the purée looks watery, drain it in a fine sieve or cheesecloth until it's thick like canned pumpkin, then measure out 1¾ cups.
Preheat the oven to 350°F and grease and parchment-line a 9×5-inch loaf pan.
Whisk the wet ingredients together — purée, eggs, oil, both sugars, and vanilla — until smooth.
In a separate bowl, whisk all the dry ingredients to distribute the spices evenly.
Fold the dry into the wet just until combined; don't overmix, or the loaf bakes up tough.
Fold in the toasted nuts, then scrape the batter into the prepared pan.
Bake 55–65 minutes until a toothpick comes out with moist crumbs (internal temperature around 205–208°F).
Cool in the pan 10 minutes, then turn out onto a wire rack to cool completely before slicing.

tips: Watery purée is the enemy of a good crumb — drain it in a sieve or cheesecloth until it mounds on a spoon like canned pumpkin.
Mix by hand; an electric mixer over-aerates the batter and leaves you with a gummy, sunken loaf.
If the top browns too fast before the center sets, tent the loaf loosely with foil for the last 15 minutes.
Toast the nuts at 350°F for 6–8 minutes first — it wakes up their oils and adds a deeper, roastier crunch.

extras.label: Make it ahead
extras.note: The loaf keeps wrapped at room temp for 3 days and freezes beautifully — slice first, then thaw a piece at a time.

extras.label: Roast extra
extras.note: One pumpkin usually yields more than 1¾ cups; freeze the surplus purée in ¼-cup portions for pancakes or oatmeal.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
Roasting your own pumpkin takes an extra half hour and pays you back tenfold — the purée
is sweeter, darker, and worlds away from the can.

recipes.title: Greek Orzo Salad with Arugula, Tomato & Red Onion
recipes.tagline: Cool orzo tossed with juicy tomatoes, peppery arugula, and a sharp red-wine vinaigrette.
recipes.pitch: A Greek salad given backbone — tender orzo folded into the crisp, fresh tangle of tomato, red onion, and peppery arugula instead of letting everything wilt in the dressing. The pasta is cooked in well-salted water, then cooled so it stays springy and drinks up just enough vinaigrette without going gluey. Everything gets dressed at the last minute, Greek-salad style, so each bite keeps its own distinct flavor and crunch. *The move is a 10-minute ice-water soak for the sliced red onion — it trades raw bite for a sweet, crisp snap that makes the whole bowl read fresh.*

items: 2 cups cherry or grape tomatoes, halved
⅓ cup Kalamata olives, pitted and halved
1 clove garlic, finely minced or grated
½ tsp Dijon mustard (optional, for emulsion)
Kosher salt and freshly cracked black pepper
Bring a pot of well-salted water to a boil and cook the orzo to al dente. Drain, rinse briefly under cool water to stop the cooking, and toss with a drizzle of olive oil so it doesn't clump.
Slice the red onion thin and soak it in a bowl of ice water for 10 minutes to mellow its bite, then drain and pat dry.
Whisk the vinaigrette ingredients together (or shake them in a jar) until emulsified. Taste and balance the salt and acid.
In a large bowl, combine the cooled orzo, tomatoes, cucumber, olives, and drained red onion.
Just before serving, add the arugula and pour over the vinaigrette. Toss gently so the greens stay crisp and everything is lightly coated.
Scatter the feta over the top, finish with cracked pepper, and serve at once.

tips: Dress this Greek-salad style — at the last minute — so the arugula stays perky and each ingredient keeps its own flavor. Dressing early softens everything into a marinated salad (still good, just different).
Salt the pasta water generously; orzo seasons from the inside as it cooks, and bland pasta is the easiest way to flatten a cold salad.
Hold a little extra vinaigrette back — chilled pasta soaks up dressing as it sits, so refresh leftovers with a splash before serving.
For a heartier bowl, add a can of drained chickpeas; they make it a full vegetarian meal.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Lean on garden tomatoes and cucumber at their peak, and tear in fresh mint or basil for a cooling, summery lift.

extras.label: Make-ahead
extras.note: Cook the orzo and mix the vinaigrette up to a day ahead, then toss with the greens and feta right before serving.

extras.label: Pairs with
extras.note: Grilled bread and a crisp rosé or a glass of Assyrtiko for a patio dinner.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
Stephen landed on a Greek salad — dressed just before serving so it stays crisp
and distinct — then made it more substantial by folding in pasta boiled in salted
water. Orzo keeps it light and forkable; orecchiette gives it more chew if you
want it.

recipes.title: Greek Salmon Salad with Lemon-Oregano Dressing
recipes.tagline: Warm oregano-rubbed salmon flaked over a classic Greek salad with a bright lemon-oregano dressing.
recipes.pitch: A classic Greek salad with the volume turned up: oregano-rubbed salmon, seared golden in a hot pan, then flaked warm over cucumber, tomato, Kalamata olives, and a generous rubble of feta. The dressing is the soul of it — good olive oil, plenty of lemon, and dried oregano whisked until it clings to every leaf. *Flake the salmon on while it's still warm so it gently wilts the greens and the feta turns just creamy at the edges — that warm-meets-cool moment is what makes it dinner.* Dried oregano carries the dish, so use the freshest jar you've got.

items: 2 salmon fillets (6–7 oz each, skin on or off)
Kosher salt and freshly ground black pepper
5 oz mixed greens (romaine, spinach, or arugula)
¼ small red onion, thinly sliced
Pinch of salt and black pepper
Whisk the olive oil, lemon juice, oregano, salt, and pepper in a small bowl or jar until emulsified. Set aside.
Halve the tomatoes, slice the cucumber and red onion, and pit the olives. Crumble the feta.
Pat the fillets very dry. Season both sides with salt, pepper, and the dried oregano.

Heat 1–2 tbsp olive oil in a skillet over medium-high until it shimmers. Sear the salmon presentation-side down (skin-side up if skin-on) and don't move it for 3–4 minutes, until a golden crust forms.
Flip gently and cook 3–4 minutes more, until it flakes easily (125–130°F internal). Rest 2 minutes, then break into large flakes.
In a large bowl, toss the greens, tomatoes, cucumber, and red onion with most of the dressing.
Divide between two plates and add the olives and feta. Top with the warm salmon flakes, drizzle the remaining dressing over the top, and garnish with dill.

tips: Dried oregano, not fresh, is the authentic Greek-salad move — it's more concentrated and perfumes the dressing better. Crush it between your fingers as you add it to wake up the oils.
Pat the salmon bone-dry before it hits the pan; surface moisture is what stops a golden crust from forming.
Press the fillet lightly with a spatula for the first 20 seconds in the pan to keep it from curling and ensure even contact.
Dress the greens just before serving so they stay crisp — limp lettuce is the one way to lose this salad.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Garden cucumber and tomatoes shine here, and a handful of torn mint or extra dill from the bed brightens every bite.

extras.label: Prefer the grill?
extras.note: Grill the oregano-rubbed fillets over medium-high, skin-side down first, about 4 minutes a side, for a smoky edge.

extras.label: Pairs with
extras.note: A crisp Assyrtiko, dry rosé, or Sauvignon Blanc, with warm olive-oiled pita alongside.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
The Greek-salad-meets-salmon version: a classic horiatiki given backbone with warm,
oregano-rubbed fish flaked on top. Pan-searing keeps it weeknight-fast and crisps
the fillet, while the lemon-oregano dressing and good feta keep it firmly,
unapologetically Greek.

recipes.title: Grilled Everything-Bagel Tuna with Creamy Horseradish Sauce & Charred Lemon Broccolini
recipes.tagline: Seared-rare tuna with a toasty sesame crust, cool horseradish cream, and smoky lemon.
recipes.pitch: Forty-five seconds a side is the whole game. The grill toasts that everything-bagel crust into a fragrant sesame-garlic shell while the center stays ruby-rare and cool — steakhouse swagger, coastal lightness. A tangy horseradish-yogurt cream sharpens every bite, and broccolini chars alongside lemon halves that go jammy and caramelized straight off the fire. *Once the tuna turns opaque it's gone, so trust the clock, not the color of the edges.*

items: 2 everything bagel–seasoned tuna steaks, about 6 oz each
½ tsp coarse salt (optional — depends on how salty the seasoning is)
½ tsp grated garlic (or a pinch of garlic powder)
Optional: red pepper flakes or a chili-oil drizzle
Heat the grill to medium-high (around 450°F) and oil the grates well so the seasoning crust doesn't tear.
Stir together the yogurt, horseradish, Dijon, lemon juice, honey, and garlic; season with salt and pepper and chill — it thickens and mellows as it rests.
Toss the broccolini with olive oil and salt. Brush the cut sides of the lemons with a little oil.
Grill the broccolini 6–8 minutes, turning occasionally, until charred and tender. Add the lemons cut-side down for the last 3 minutes until caramelized.
Move the broccolini and lemons to a warm plate and tent loosely with foil.
Brush the tuna lightly with olive oil. Grill 45–60 seconds per side for rare (ruby center), or up to 90 seconds for medium-rare. Rotate halfway through each side if you want crosshatch marks.
Rest the steaks 2 minutes — carryover heat finishes the sear without cooking the center.
Smear a generous spoonful of horseradish cream onto each plate and set the tuna on top or slightly offset.
Add the broccolini, squeeze over the charred lemon, and finish with flaky salt and an optional drizzle of chili oil.

tips: The second tuna turns opaque all the way through it goes chalky — pull it while the center is still rosy and let carryover do the rest.
Flip with a fish spatula or tongs, gently; the everything-bagel crust wants to grab the grates.
Charred lemons get sweeter and more aromatic — they're for squeezing and for looks, so don't skip the grill marks.
Toss a small chunk of cedar or fruitwood on the coals for a minute before the tuna goes on for a whisper of smoke.

extras.label: Make it a fuller plate
extras.note: Round it out with sesame jasmine rice or grilled smashed potatoes; otherwise keep it lean and high-protein as written.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: A few thin slices of garden jalapeño or serrano stirred into the horseradish cream adds a clean background heat, and torn basil over the top brightens the plate.

extras.label: Pairs with
extras.note: A crisp New Zealand Sauvignon Blanc or a minerally Sancerre. For a cocktail, a citrus gin martini with cucumber echoes the brightness.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
Those pre-seasoned tuna steaks are a head start to a fast, impressive dinner: the
grill toasts the crust, the center stays rare, and a cool horseradish cream plays
the role of a steakhouse sauce gone coastal. Char the lemons hard — they're half
the flavor and all of the drama.

recipes.title: Grilled Fish Tacos with Lime Crema & Quick Slaw
recipes.tagline: Chili-lime grilled white fish in warm tortillas with crunchy slaw and smoky lime crema.
recipes.pitch: Lighter than the beer-battered Baja version but every bit as good: firm white fish takes a quick chili-lime marinade and a fast char on the grill, then folds into warm tortillas with a crisp cabbage-lime slaw and a cool, tangy crema. *All the freshness of a beach taco with none of the fryer — the char does the heavy lifting.* It plugs straight into the salsas already in this book and comes together in under half an hour.

items: Lime wedges, cilantro, and hot sauce
Whisk the oil, lime juice, chili powder, cumin, garlic, and salt. Coat the fish and marinate 15 minutes.
Toss the cabbage with cilantro, lime juice, oil, and salt. Stir together the crema ingredients.
Heat the grill (or a grill pan) to medium-high and oil the grates. Grill the fish 3–4 minutes per side, until charred and just flaking, then break into big chunks.
Warm the tortillas. Layer slaw, fish, and avocado, drizzle with lime crema, and finish with cotija, cilantro, lime, and a shake of hot sauce.

tips: A fish basket or well-oiled grates keep delicate fillets from sticking; if in doubt, sear them in cast iron instead.
Don't over-marinate — 15 minutes is plenty, or the lime starts to "cook" the fish before it hits the grill.
Double-stack (or char) the tortillas so they don't fall apart under the juicy fish.
Want it crispy? Dredge the fish in seasoned flour and pan-fry instead of grilling for the battered Baja version.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: A handful of garden cilantro and a sliced serrano or jalapeño for heat.

extras.label: Salsa it up
extras.note: Spoon on the chunky mango-habanero or creamy jalapeño-cilantro salsa already in this book.

extras.label: Make it a bowl
extras.note: Skip the tortillas and pile everything over rice or greens for a taco bowl.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
The taco that conquered Southern California, in its lighter grilled form. Hot charred fish
against cold crunchy slaw is the entire point — and the crispy variation is one tip away.

recipes.title: Grilled Leafy Greens with Spicy Garlic Oil
recipes.tagline: Hearty greens massaged in chili-garlic oil and charred fast on the grill.
recipes.pitch: Sturdy greens were made for fire. Garlic and crushed red pepper are brought to a gentle boil in olive oil until the cloves go golden and the oil turns fragrant and faintly spicy, then that oil does double duty — you massage it right into the leaves before they hit a hot grill. A minute a side is all it takes for blistered, smoky, silky chard with frizzled edges. It's the side that quietly steals the plate. *Massaging the warm oil in is the move — it seasons and softens the greens before the grill ever touches them.*

items: ¼ tsp crushed red pepper flakes
¼ tsp kosher salt, plus more
1 large bunch Swiss chard (or Tuscan kale), tough stems trimmed
Prepare a grill for medium-high heat.
Bring the garlic, olive oil, red pepper flakes, and ¼ tsp salt to a boil in a small saucepan over medium heat. Cook until the garlic is golden, then let cool; season with more salt and black pepper.
Combine the greens and the garlic oil in a large bowl, massaging the oil into the leaves until they're glossy and slightly softened.
Grill the greens until lightly charred and tender, about 1 minute per side. Pile onto a platter and spoon over any oil and garlic left in the bowl.

tips: No grill? Char the oiled greens in a screaming-hot cast-iron pan or under the broiler — you're after blistered, frizzled edges either way.
Don't toss the chard stems — slice them thin and grill alongside, or save for a quick pickle.
Make the spicy garlic oil ahead — it keeps for days and is great on eggs, beans, or toast.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: A handful of garden arugula tossed in raw at the end adds a fresh, peppery contrast to the smoky leaves.

extras.label: Make it a meal
extras.note: Pile over white beans or thick yogurt with grilled bread, or serve alongside any simple fish.

source.name: Bon Appétit — Chris Morocco

recipes.headnote:
Chris Morocco's trick for hearty greens: bloom garlic and chili in olive oil, massage
it into the leaves, then char them fast over high heat. Smoky, silky, and just spicy
enough.

recipes.title: Grilled Lemon-Herb Chicken with Garden Vegetables
recipes.tagline: Lemon-and-rosemary marinated chicken grilled alongside charred summer vegetables.
recipes.pitch: Proof that simple and healthy can still taste like the best night of summer. Chicken breasts soak in a bright marinade of lemon, garlic, rosemary, and thyme until they're juicy and herb-perfumed, then hit a hot grill for smoky char marks. Skewers of zucchini, bell pepper, and cherry tomatoes cook right alongside, blistering and going sweet over the flame. A final squeeze of lemon over everything pulls the whole plate together. *Pound the breasts to an even thickness before they go on — that's the difference between juicy all the way through and dry, leathery edges.*

items: 2 boneless skinless chicken breasts, pounded to even thickness
1 tbsp fresh rosemary, chopped (from the garden)
1 tbsp fresh thyme leaves (from the garden)
½ tsp freshly ground black pepper
A little extra chopped rosemary or thyme
Whisk the olive oil, lemon juice and zest, garlic, rosemary, thyme, salt, and pepper. Add the chicken and turn to coat. Marinate 30 minutes to 2 hours in the fridge.
Heat the grill to medium-high and oil the grates. Thread the zucchini, pepper, and cherry tomatoes onto skewers and brush with olive oil, salt, and pepper.
Grill the chicken 5–6 minutes per side until char marks form and the internal temperature reaches 160°F. Rest 5 minutes (carryover brings it to 165°F).
Grill the vegetable skewers alongside, turning, until charred and tender — about 3–4 minutes per side; pull the tomatoes as soon as they blister.
Slice the chicken and plate with the grilled vegetables. Squeeze fresh lemon over everything and finish with flaky salt and a scatter of extra herbs.

tips: Pound the breasts to an even thickness so they cook through at the same rate and don't dry out at the thin ends.
Pull the chicken at 160°F and let it rest — carryover heat finishes it at a safe, juicy 165°F.
Don't over-marinate: the lemon's acid starts to firm up the surface of the chicken past a couple of hours, so 30 minutes to 2 hours is the sweet spot.
Oil the grates right before the chicken goes on to prevent sticking and tearing the char you worked for.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Rosemary and thyme straight from the garden make the marinade; toss a sliced serrano onto the skewers if you want a little heat.

extras.label: Swap the veg
extras.note: Asparagus, red onion wedges, or corn on the cob all grill beautifully in place of (or alongside) the zucchini and peppers.

extras.label: Pairs with
extras.note: A chilled white like Pinot Grigio, or sparkling water with a lot of lemon for a clean summer plate.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
The grilled chicken Stephen comes back to all summer: a lemon-and-herb marinade
does the heavy lifting, and the garden vegetables char right alongside on
skewers for a clean, healthy plate that still tastes like a cookout.

recipes.title: Grilled New York Strip with Charred Corn
recipes.tagline: A two-zone grilled strip with buttery, blistered corn on the cob for two.
recipes.pitch: Summer dinner, dialed in: build a two-zone fire and let the grill do two jobs at once. The corn steams in foil over the cooler side while the strips sear over the coals, picking up a charred, smoky crust and clean grill marks. When the steaks hit medium-rare you slide them to the cool zone to coast, rest under foil, then finish the corn with a fresh pat of butter that melts into the kernels. *The two-zone setup is the move — one side to sear hard, one side to coast, so nothing flares or overcooks while you tend the other.*

items: 2 New York strip steaks (1–1.5 inches thick)
Kosher salt and freshly ground black pepper
Optional: garlic powder, smoked paprika, or fresh rosemary/thyme sprigs
2 tbsp butter, plus more for finishing
Optional: chopped cilantro or grated Parmesan, for serving
Pat the steaks dry and let them sit at room temperature 20–30 minutes. Coat lightly with oil and season generously with salt and pepper (and any optional spices).
Rub each ear of corn with butter, season with salt and pepper, and wrap tightly in foil, sealing the ends to trap steam.
Preheat the grill to medium-high, around 400–450°F. Bank the coals (or set burners) for a two-zone fire — one hot side for searing, one cooler side for indirect cooking.
Set the foil-wrapped corn on the cooler side first. Grill 15–20 minutes, turning every 5 minutes, until the kernels are tender and lightly caramelized.
Lay the steaks over the hottest part of the grill and sear with the lid closed, 3–4 minutes per side. For crosshatch marks, rotate each steak 45 degrees halfway through each side.
If they need more time to reach doneness, slide them to the cooler side. Pull at 130–135°F for medium-rare (rare 120–125°F, medium 140–145°F).
Rest the steaks on a plate, tented loosely with foil, for 5–10 minutes. Add a pat of garlic-herb butter while they rest if you like.
Carefully unwrap the corn and add an extra pat of butter to each ear; sprinkle with cilantro or Parmesan if using.
Plate each steak with an ear of corn and finish with cracked pepper and flaky salt.

tips: For clean crosshatch grill marks, rotate the steak 45 degrees halfway through each side rather than flipping repeatedly.
Start the corn before the steaks — it needs the full 15–20 minutes, while the strips only want a few minutes a side over high heat.
A pat of garlic-herb butter added as the steak rests melts down and doubles as a built-in sauce.
Pull the steaks 5°F below target; carryover heat finishes them under the foil tent so they land right at medium-rare.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Toss a few rosemary or thyme sprigs onto the coals while searing for aromatic smoke, and finish the corn with chopped garden cilantro.

extras.label: Make it street-corn
extras.note: Brush the grilled corn with mayo, dust with chili powder and lime, and finish with cotija for an elote-style side.

extras.label: Pairs with
extras.note: A crisp lager or a juicy Zinfandel — both stand up to char and butter on a warm night.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
The classic backyard pairing, run like a line cook would: corn on the cool zone,
steaks on the fire, and a two-zone setup that keeps everything moving without a
single flare-up. Pull the steaks early and let the foil tent finish them.

recipes.title: Grilled Salmon Salad with Dijon-Balsamic Vinaigrette
recipes.tagline: Warm grilled salmon over cool spring mix, garden cucumber and tomato, with a tangy Dijon-balsamic vinaigrette.
recipes.pitch: Warm fish, cool greens — that contrast is the whole pleasure. Salmon goes straight over a medium-high grill, skin-side down, and stays put until it lifts cleanly with a just-pink center, then rests while you toss spring mix, garden cucumber, and burst-sweet cherry tomatoes in a sharp Dijon-balsamic vinaigrette. Kalamata olives and crumbled feta push it Mediterranean; thin-sliced red onion, soaked to mellow, keeps it bright. *Lay the salmon on warm so it half-wilts the greens beneath it — the dressing clings, the leaves soften just so, and every forkful gets fish, crunch, and tang at once.*

items: 2 salmon fillets (6–7 oz each, skin on or off)
Kosher salt and cracked black pepper
½ large cucumber, thinly sliced (peeled if thick-skinned)
¼ small red onion, very thinly sliced
¼–⅓ cup Kalamata olives, pitted and halved
1 tsp honey or maple syrup (optional, for balance)
Pinch of salt and black pepper
Whisk the Dijon, balsamic, and a pinch of salt and pepper in a small bowl or jar; add honey if you want a softer edge. Slowly drizzle in the olive oil while whisking until emulsified. Set aside.
Halve the tomatoes and olives. Thinly slice the cucumber and red onion (a mandoline gets them paper-thin). Soak the onion in ice water for 10 minutes to mellow its bite, then drain. Crumble the feta.
Heat the grill to medium-high and oil the grates. Brush the salmon with olive oil and season generously with salt and pepper.

Grill skin-side down first and don't move it for about 4 minutes, until it releases cleanly. Flip and cook 3–4 minutes more, until it flakes easily with a just-pink center (125–130°F internal). Rest 2 minutes.
Toss the spring mix, tomatoes, cucumber, and red onion with a little of the vinaigrette and divide between two shallow bowls or plates.
Set the salmon on top, whole or flaked into large chunks. Scatter the olives and feta, drizzle more vinaigrette over the fish, and serve right away while it's warm.

tips: Let the salmon tell you when to flip — it sticks until the skin crisps and releases, then lifts cleanly off the grates with no tearing.
Soaking the sliced red onion in ice water for 10 minutes tames the raw bite and crisps it up.
A lidded jar emulsifies the vinaigrette better than a whisk and stores the extra; shake hard right before dressing.
Dress the greens lightly and save the bolder drizzle for over the warm salmon, where it pools and clings.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Garden cucumber and cherry tomatoes are the backbone here; a basil or mint chiffonade torn over the top adds a fresh, herby lift.

extras.label: No grill tonight?
extras.note: Pan-sear the patted-dry fillets in a hot skillet, presentation-side down, 3–4 minutes per side to 125–130°F — or bake at 400°F for 12–15 minutes and broil the last minute for color.

extras.label: Pairs with
extras.note: A chilled Sauvignon Blanc, dry rosé, or Albariño. Toast olive-oiled pita or baguette for a crisp side.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
A dinner salad that came together from a full garden haul: cucumber and cherry
tomatoes from the bed, spring mix and red onion from the store, and a Dijon-
balsamic vinaigrette shaken in a jar. Kalamata olives and feta nudge it
Mediterranean, and the warm grilled salmon over cool greens is the contrast that
makes it dinner, not a side.

recipes.title: Hainanese Chicken Rice
recipes.tagline: Gently poached silky chicken with fragrant schmaltz rice and a trio of bright sauces.
recipes.pitch: The "national dish of Singapore," and a genuine technique to add to your kit: a whole chicken poached to silky, just-set tenderness, then the rice cooked in the chicken-rich poaching broth so every grain tastes like the bird. It comes with a trio of sauces — fiery chili-garlic, bright ginger-scallion, and dark soy. *It looks plain and tastes like the most comforting thing you've ever made — proof that poaching, done right, beats roasting for pure tenderness.* Restrained, elegant, and unlike anything else on the menu.
