items: Rub the chicken with the salt. Put it in a pot with the ginger, scallions, and smashed garlic and cover with cold water. Bring to a bare simmer, then cook very gently 30–35 minutes (or turn off the heat and steep, covered, 45 minutes), until it reaches 165°F in the thigh.
Lift the chicken out and plunge it into ice water for 5 minutes to set the skin. Reserve the poaching broth.
Heat the oil in a pot and cook the garlic and ginger 1 minute. Add the rinsed rice and toast 2 minutes.
Add 2½ cups of the poaching broth, bring to a boil, cover, and cook on low 15 minutes. Rest, covered, 10 minutes, then fluff.
Ginger-scallion oil: combine the scallion, ginger, and salt in a heatproof bowl. Heat the oil until shimmering and pour it over so it sizzles.
Chili sauce: blend the serranos, garlic, ginger, lime juice, sugar, and broth until smooth.
Carve the chicken and serve over or alongside the rice with cucumber, cilantro, the two sauces, and a drizzle of dark soy.

tips: Poach at a bare shiver, never a rolling boil — that's the secret to silky, just-set meat instead of stringy.
The ice bath right after poaching sets the skin into its signature glossy, slippery texture.
Cook the rice in the poaching broth, not water — that's where all the chicken flavor lives.
The three sauces are the dish — don't skip the ginger-scallion oil or the chili, and make extra of both.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Garden serranos or jalapeños in the chili sauce, and cilantro over everything.

extras.label: Short on time
extras.note: A few bone-in chicken thighs poach faster than a whole bird and still make a rich broth.

extras.label: Make it a spread
extras.note: A small bowl of the hot poaching broth with sliced scallion alongside is traditional.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
Deceptively simple and deeply technique-driven: the gentlest poach, the most fragrant rice,
and three sauces that turn a plain plate into something you'll crave.

recipes.title: Herb-Butter Swordfish
recipes.tagline: Meaty swordfish steaks basted in a melting garden-herb compound butter.
recipes.pitch: Swordfish is the steak of the sea, and it wants to be treated like one: a hot pan, a golden crust, and a slick of butter to finish. These thick steaks sear until they're bronzed on both faces, then get basted in a foaming compound butter loaded with garden parsley, dill, and chives until every bite tastes of herb and brown butter. Garlicky roasted Brussels sprouts with a flick of lemon zest round it into a full plate. *Tilt the pan and spoon that herb butter over the fish for the last minute — that's the whole trick to a steakhouse finish.*

items: 2 tbsp unsalted butter, plus more for basting
2 tbsp mixed fresh herbs (parsley, dill, chives), finely chopped
Salt and freshly ground black pepper, to taste
1 lb Brussels sprouts, trimmed and halved
½ tsp red pepper flakes (adjust to taste)
Salt and freshly ground black pepper
Preheat the oven to 400°F. Toss the halved Brussels sprouts with the minced garlic, olive oil, red pepper flakes, and a generous pinch of salt; spread in a single layer on a sheet pan.
Roast 20–25 minutes, stirring once halfway, until crispy at the edges and tender inside. Toss with lemon zest and black pepper the moment they come out.
Soften 2 tbsp butter and mash in the chopped herbs and garlic with a pinch of salt and pepper. Set aside (it'll melt and foam in the pan when you baste).
Pat the steaks dry and season both sides with salt and pepper. Heat the olive oil in a cast-iron skillet over medium-high.
Lay the steaks in the hot pan and cook undisturbed 3–4 minutes per side, until a golden crust forms and the fish is just opaque through (thickness dictates the exact time).
In the last minute, drop the herb butter into the pan. As it foams, tilt the skillet and spoon the herby brown butter over the fish again and again.
Plate the swordfish with the roasted Brussels sprouts, pour over any pan butter, and serve with lemon wedges.

tips: Swordfish dries out fast — pull it the moment it turns opaque in the center; it should still feel springy, not firm.
A dry surface is everything for the crust; blot the steaks well and don't crowd the pan.
Build the compound butter ahead and chill it in a log; slice off coins to melt over the fish right at the table.
If grilling instead, oil the grates and cook over medium-high 3–4 minutes per side, then finish with a coin of the herb butter melting on top.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Use whatever's thriving — parsley, dill, chives, even a little thyme or basil — in the compound butter, and the lemon zest from a garden Meyer lemon if you've got one.

extras.label: Side swaps
extras.note: From the same source trio, a grilled corn and bell pepper salad (lime-honey vinaigrette) or a spicy corn and Brussels sprout sauté both pair beautifully if you want something brighter than the roast.

extras.label: Pairs with
extras.note: A medium-bodied white like a lightly oaked Chardonnay or a dry rosé stands up to the meaty fish and brown butter.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
Swordfish takes to a sear like a good steak, and a garden-herb compound butter
spooned over the top in the last minute turns it into something restaurant-worthy.
Garlicky roasted Brussels sprouts with lemon zest make it a complete, easy plate
for two.

recipes.title: Korean Gochujang-Glazed Salmon with Sesame Bok Choy
recipes.tagline: Broiled salmon lacquered in sweet-spicy gochujang over fast sesame-charred bok choy.
recipes.pitch: Gochujang is the sweet-savory-spicy note most weeknight salmon is missing, and here it turns into a glaze that caramelizes into a sticky, blistered crust. The fillets get coated, broiled until the edges char and bubble, and finished with a second brush of glaze for shine, over quick sesame-charred bok choy. *Run it under the broiler for the last minute and the glaze goes lacquered and blackened-in-spots — the best part.* It's on the table in twenty-odd minutes and built on the salmon you cook all the time.

items: Whisk the gochujang, honey, soy, rice vinegar, sesame oil, garlic, and ginger. Pat the salmon dry and coat it with half the glaze; reserve the rest for finishing. Let it sit 10 minutes if you have time.

Heat the broiler with a rack in the upper third and line a sheet pan with foil. Broil the salmon 6–8 minutes, until the glaze caramelizes and the fish reaches 125–130°F, brushing on the reserved glaze halfway through.
Meanwhile, heat the neutral and sesame oils in a skillet over medium-high. Add the garlic, then the bok choy cut-side down, and sear 2 minutes.
Splash in the soy sauce and a tablespoon of water, cover, and steam 2 minutes more, until crisp-tender.
Plate the salmon and bok choy over rice, and finish with scallions and sesame seeds.

tips: Reserve half the glaze for the end — gochujang scorches fast, so the second coat goes on after the heat for shine without burning.
Broil on the upper rack and watch it; you want lacquered, blistered edges, not blackened ones.
Pull the salmon at 125°F for silky or 130°F for firm — it keeps cooking off the heat.
Heat dial: gochujang brands vary a lot — start at 2 tbsp and add a pinch of gochugaru or a squeeze of sriracha if you want more burn.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: A shower of garden scallion and a few slices of serrano amp up the fresh heat.

extras.label: Make it a bowl
extras.note: Pile everything over rice with sliced cucumber and a soft egg for a gochujang salmon rice bowl.

extras.label: Swap
extras.note: The glaze works on any firm fish — try it on cod or black cod.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
A fast, fiery weeknight salmon that leans on one great Korean pantry staple — and proves
the broiler is the most underused tool for a caramelized glaze.

recipes.title: Lemon-Caper Deviled Eggs
recipes.tagline: Classic deviled eggs brightened with lemon zest, capers, and grated garlic.
recipes.pitch: Deviled eggs are a party staple for a reason, and this version earns its spot with a hit of brightness — lemon zest and rinsed capers cut the richness of the yolk-and-mayo filling, while a whisper of grated garlic keeps it savory. Whir the filling smooth in a food processor, pipe it back into the whites from a snipped plastic bag, and dust with paprika and parsley. Make the parts a couple days ahead and assemble at the last minute. *Zest and capers are the upgrade — that little jolt of acid and brine is what makes people reach for a second.*

items: ¼ tsp freshly ground black pepper
Bring a large saucepan of water to a boil and lower the eggs in one at a time with a ladle. Reduce to a low boil and cook 10 minutes, then drain and cool.
Peel the cooled eggs and slice lengthwise. Pop the yolks into a food processor and arrange the whites cut-side up on a serving platter.
Pulse the yolks alone for about 10 seconds. Add the mayonnaise, mustard, capers, garlic, lemon zest, salt, and pepper, and process until smooth, scraping down the sides as needed.
Scrape the filling into a resealable plastic bag, seal it, and snip off a corner. Pipe the filling into the egg whites.
Garnish with a dusting of paprika and a sprinkle of chopped parsley just before serving.

tips: Make ahead by keeping the piped filling and empty whites in separate covered containers for up to two days, then pipe and garnish right before the party.
Slightly older eggs (a week or two) peel far more cleanly than fresh ones.
For an even silkier filling, push the yolks through a fine sieve before adding the mayo.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Swap the parsley garnish for snipped chives, dill, or fresh mint from the garden.

extras.label: Dress it up
extras.note: For a fancier plate, top each with a tiny caper berry, a curl of lemon zest, or a pinch of smoked paprika.

source.name: Food Network — Katie Lee Biegel

recipes.headnote:
Katie Lee's bright take on a classic: lemon zest and capers fold into a smooth,
food-processor-easy yolk filling. Make the components ahead and pipe them in just
before serving.

recipes.title: Maple Pumpkin-Spice Pepitas
recipes.tagline: Hulled pumpkin seeds tossed with maple and pumpkin spice, roasted crisp.
recipes.pitch: These are the snack you make once and keep reaching for — raw green pepitas tossed with a little oil, real maple syrup, and warm pumpkin pie spice, then roasted low and slow until they're glossy, toasty, and shatter-crisp. The maple caramelizes into the barest candied shell while the spice does its cozy fall thing. Scatter them on oatmeal, yogurt bowls, and salads, or just eat them by the handful. *They crisp up as they cool, so pull them while they still look slightly soft — that's exactly right.*

items: 2 tsp olive oil (or melted coconut oil)
Preheat the oven to 325°F and line a baking sheet with parchment.
In a bowl, toss the pepitas with the oil, maple syrup, pumpkin pie spice, and salt until evenly coated.
Spread in a single layer on the baking sheet and roast 12–15 minutes, stirring once halfway, until golden and fragrant.
Let cool completely on the pan — they'll crisp up as they cool. Store in an airtight jar.

tips: Watch the last few minutes closely; the maple sugar can go from toasty to scorched quickly.
Add a pinch of cayenne or chipotle for a sweet-heat version that's great on soups and salads.
Pepitas (hulled, green) roast faster than in-shell pumpkin seeds — if you start from whole roasting-pumpkin seeds, rinse, dry, and roast at 300°F for about 45 minutes instead.

extras.label: Use them everywhere
extras.note: Sprinkle over oatmeal, yogurt bowls, roasted squash, or a fall salad for instant crunch.

extras.label: Make a batch
extras.note: Doubles or triples easily on two sheet pans — they keep in an airtight container for a couple of weeks.

source.name: Eating Bird Food — Brittany Mullins

recipes.headnote:
Brittany Mullins' cozy fall move — pumpkin pie spice and a drizzle of maple — applied to
quick-roasting hulled pepitas. Toss, roast, and cool to crisp; then put them on
everything.

recipes.title: Mediterranean Fresh Tuna with Blistered Tomatoes, Olives & Basil
recipes.tagline: Seared tuna simmered in a quick garlic-tomato sauce with Kalamata and torn basil.
recipes.pitch: This is weeknight Mediterranean cooking at its most honest: golden garlic bloomed slowly in good olive oil, then cherry tomatoes piled in until their skins split and go jammy and saucy. Cubes of fresh tuna slip in at the end with oregano, Kalamata olives, and torn basil, just long enough to drink up the sauce. It comes together in one pan in about 25 minutes and tastes like the coast. *The trick is patience with the garlic — take it to gold, not brown, so the whole sauce turns sweet instead of bitter.*

items: 4 fresh basil leaves, plus more torn for serving
Salt and freshly ground black pepper, to taste
Cut the tuna into 1-inch cubes. Quarter the tomatoes and mince the garlic.
Warm the olive oil with the garlic in a skillet over low heat, stirring, until the garlic turns pale gold and fragrant — don't let it brown.
Add the tomatoes, season with salt and pepper, raise to medium-high, and cook about 5 minutes, until they collapse into a loose sauce.
Stir in the tuna, oregano, olives, and a few basil leaves. Cover and cook 10–15 minutes, until the tuna is cooked through and has soaked up the sauce.
Taste and adjust seasoning, finish with freshly torn basil and a grind of black pepper, and serve.

tips: For rosy, sushi-grade tuna, skip the covered simmer — sear the cubes 30–45 seconds per side at the end and just spoon the warm tomato sauce over them.
Spoon any leftover sauce over couscous, orzo, rice, or grilled bread; it's too good to leave in the pan.
A splash of dry white wine or a squeeze of lemon at the tomato stage brightens the whole dish.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Garden cherry tomatoes and a fistful of fresh basil make this sing; a little chopped oregano or thyme works too.

extras.label: Make it a meal
extras.note: Serve over orzo or with crusty bread and a simple arugula salad.

source.name: SideChef — The Fit Mediterranean

recipes.headnote:
A fast, sunny one-pan supper: fresh tuna gently cooked in a garlicky tomato sauce
with Kalamata olives and basil. Take the garlic only to gold and the sauce stays
sweet — and if you like your tuna pink, just sear it and spoon the sauce over.

recipes.title: Mediterranean Stuffed Peppers with Quinoa, Chickpeas & Feta
recipes.tagline: Sweet roasted bell peppers packed with herbed quinoa, chickpeas, and melty feta.
recipes.pitch: Bell peppers turn silky and sweet in the oven, the perfect vessel for a filling of nutty quinoa, creamy chickpeas, and salty feta laced with oregano and lemon. The trick is to pre-roast the empty pepper halves so they soften and concentrate before they're stuffed — no raw, squeaky pepper, no watery base. The filling is built to stand on its own, brightened with lemon and fresh herbs so it tastes like more than the sum of its pantry parts. A final blast under the heat melts the feta into golden pockets across the top. *Toasting the quinoa dry in the pan before it simmers is the quiet upgrade — it deepens everything into something warm and nutty rather than flat.*

items: 2 large bell peppers (red, yellow, or orange), halved and seeded
Kosher salt and freshly cracked black pepper
1 cup canned chickpeas, drained and rinsed
1 small shallot or ¼ red onion, finely diced
Juice and zest of ½ lemon
2 tbsp chopped fresh parsley (plus more to finish)
Preheat the oven to 400°F. Rub the pepper halves inside and out with olive oil and season with salt and pepper.
Set them cut-side up in a baking dish and roast 15 minutes until they begin to soften and slump. Leave the oven on.

Toast the rinsed quinoa in a dry saucepan over medium heat for 2–3 minutes until it smells nutty. Add the broth, bring to a boil, then cover and simmer 12–15 minutes until the liquid is absorbed. Fluff with a fork.
In a skillet, warm 1 tbsp olive oil over medium heat and soften the shallot 2–3 minutes. Add the garlic, oregano, and cumin and cook 1 minute until fragrant.
Stir in the chickpeas, tomatoes, and olives and cook 3–4 minutes until the tomatoes start to break down.
Fold in the cooked quinoa, lemon juice and zest, parsley, and half the feta. Taste and adjust salt, pepper, and red pepper flakes.
Spoon the filling generously into the roasted pepper halves and scatter the remaining feta over the top.
Return to the oven and bake 18–22 minutes, until the peppers are tender and the feta is golden in spots.
Finish with fresh parsley and serve with lemon wedges.

tips: Pre-roasting the empty peppers before stuffing is the difference between sweet and tender versus raw and watery — don't skip it.
Rinse the quinoa even if the package says pre-rinsed; it washes off the bitter saponin coating that can make the filling taste soapy.
These reheat beautifully, so make the full batch — the filling tastes even better the next day once the flavors settle.
For golden, bubbling tops, finish under the broiler for the last 2 minutes; watch closely so the feta doesn't scorch.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Use garden cherry tomatoes and a handful of chopped fresh mint or basil in the filling; a little fresh oregano in place of dried sings here.

extras.label: Make it vegan
extras.note: Swap the feta for a few spoonfuls of dairy-free feta or a dollop of seasoned cashew cream stirred through the warm filling.

extras.label: Pairs with
extras.note: A simple cucumber-yogurt sauce or tzatziki on the side, and a glass of dry rosé or a light red like Grenache.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
Of the vegetarian Mediterranean options Stephen weighed, the stuffed peppers were
the one to build out fully: a one-dish dinner that holds together, travels well as
leftovers, and leans on pantry staples brightened with lemon and herbs.

recipes.title: Miso-Glazed Black Cod with Charred Bok Choy & Ginger-Scallion Oil
recipes.tagline: Nobu-style lacquered cod, smoky bok choy, and a sizzling aromatic oil.
recipes.pitch: This is the dish that makes people think you trained somewhere fancy. Buttery black cod sits overnight in a sweet-savory miso marinade, then caramelizes under the broiler until the edges go deep amber and the flesh flakes into silky sheets. Charred baby bok choy and a hot-poured ginger-scallion oil bring smoke, freshness, and a little heat, while quick pickled cucumbers cut clean through the richness. *The marinade does the work the night before — twenty minutes from fridge to plate is all that's left.*

items: 2 black cod (sablefish) fillets, about 6 oz each
½ English cucumber (or 2 Persian), thinly sliced
Toasted sesame seeds and cilantro, to garnish

Whisk the miso, mirin, sake, soy sauce, and honey until smooth. Pat the cod dry, coat it evenly in the marinade, and refrigerate at least 4 hours, ideally overnight. Black cod is rich and forgiving — longer means deeper umami.
Stir the rice vinegar, sugar, and salt until dissolved, then toss with the cucumber and sesame seeds. Let sit at least 15 minutes (longer is better).

Combine the scallions, ginger, garlic, chili flakes, and sesame oil in a heatproof bowl. Heat the neutral oil until shimmering and pour it over the aromatics — it should sizzle. Stir, salt to taste, and set aside to bloom.
Position a rack in the upper third and preheat the broiler on high. Line a sheet with foil and lightly oil it. Wipe excess marinade off the fillets, leaving a thin coating to prevent scorching.
Broil 8–10 minutes until nearly cooked through, watching closely — miso burns fast, so a light char is the target, not black. The cod is done when it flakes easily and the edges are glossy amber.
Brush the bok choy halves with neutral oil. Sear cut-side down in a hot skillet (or on the grill) 2–3 minutes until charred, flip for 1 minute more, and finish with flaky salt.
Fan the bok choy on each plate and spoon over the ginger-scallion oil. Lay the cod slightly offset, glazed side up.
Nest a small pile of pickled cucumber alongside (or on a mound of rice), then finish with sesame seeds and a few cilantro leaves.

tips: Miso scorches in seconds under a broiler — leave only a thin film on the fish and stay at the oven door.
No mirin? Mix 1 tbsp white wine with 1 tsp sugar and 1 tsp water until dissolved; it works perfectly in the marinade.
Don't overcook the bok choy — its crunch and vivid green are what make the plate pop against the rich fish.
Make extra pickled cucumber; spooned over a bite of cod, the acidity is the contrast the whole plate is built on.

extras.label: Sea bass on the grill
extras.note: Sea bass is a great alternative — marinate just 10–15 minutes (it's leaner), then grill skin-side down 3–4 minutes, flip, brush with glaze, and grill 3–4 minutes more. Move it to indirect heat after searing if the honey-miso starts to scorch.

extras.label: Regular cod swap
extras.note: Standard cod works too — it's leaner, so marinate no longer than 12 hours and shorten the broil to 6–8 minutes plus a 2-minute char so it stays moist.

extras.label: Restaurant finishing drizzle
extras.note: For extra shine, melt 1 tbsp butter and stir in 1 tsp each soy, honey, and rice vinegar plus a pinch of grated ginger; drizzle this ginger-soy butter over the fish right before serving.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Finish with garden cilantro, and use a garden cucumber for the quick pickle. A pinch of jalapeño in the pickling liquid adds a gentle background heat.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
Restaurant elegance from a make-ahead marinade: the miso does its quiet work
overnight, so the night-of is just a fast broil, a hot sizzle of ginger-scallion
oil, and a charred green. The pickled cucumber is the secret — bright acidity to
cut the buttery, lacquered cod.

recipes.title: Moroccan Chicken Tagine with Green Olives & Preserved Lemon
recipes.tagline: Spice-rubbed chicken thighs braised with saffron, green olives, and preserved lemon over couscous.
recipes.pitch: A new cuisine built from flavors you already reach for — olives, lemon, warm spice. Chicken thighs get a cumin-paprika-ginger rub and a hard sear, then braise low with onion, saffron, cracked green olives, and preserved lemon until they're falling off the bone in a glossy, briny sauce. *The preserved lemon is the whole magic trick — it goes silky and floral and makes the entire pot taste expensive.* It's a forgiving one-pot braise that scales up, reheats even better, and lands over buttery couscous.

items: ¾ cup green olives (Castelvetrano or cracked)
Pat the thighs dry. Mix the cumin, paprika, ground ginger, turmeric, cinnamon, salt, and pepper, and rub it all over the chicken.
Heat the oil in a Dutch oven over medium-high. Brown the thighs skin-side down 5–6 minutes until golden, flip for 2 minutes, then remove.
Lower the heat and cook the onion 5 minutes until soft. Add the garlic, fresh ginger, coriander, and saffron and cook 1 minute.
Pour in the broth and scrape up the browned bits. Nestle the chicken back in skin-side up and scatter the preserved lemon and olives around it.
Cover and simmer gently (or braise in a 350°F oven) 35–40 minutes, until the chicken is tender and nearly falling off the bone. Uncover for the last 10 minutes to reduce the sauce.
Shower with the cilantro and parsley and serve over couscous, spooning the briny sauce on top.

tips: Use only the rind of the preserved lemon — scrape away and discard the soft, intensely salty pulp, then slice the rind into thin ribbons.
Brown the thighs hard and don't crowd the pan; that fond is the backbone of the sauce.
The salt comes mostly from the olives and preserved lemon, so hold off on adding more until the very end.
Like most braises, it's even better the next day — cool, chill, and reheat gently.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: A big handful of garden cilantro and parsley at the finish; a little thyme in the braise.

extras.label: Scale it
extras.note: A forgiving braise — halve it for a dinner for two, or double it in a bigger pot for a crowd.

extras.label: Pairs with
extras.note: Buttery couscous or warm flatbread and a chopped cucumber-tomato salad.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
The classic Moroccan pairing — salt-cured lemon and green olives against saffron and warm
spice — does all the work here. Bone-in thighs stay juicy through the long braise and make
their own rich sauce.

recipes.title: Pan-Seared Halibut with Charred Corn & Cherry-Tomato Succotash
recipes.tagline: A crisp-seared halibut fillet over warm charred-corn succotash, finished with nutty brown butter.
recipes.pitch: Halibut is the meatiest, most luxurious of the white fish, and the move here is a hard cast-iron sear that lacquers the top golden while the inside stays just-set and silky. It lands on a warm succotash of blistered summer corn, jammy cherry tomatoes, shallot, and basil, then gets a spoonful of brown butter poured over at the table. *The whole trick is pulling it at 130°F so it flakes in big translucent petals instead of drying to cotton.* It's a thirty-minute, one-pan dinner that eats like the coast.

items: 2 ears corn, kernels cut off (about 1½ cups)
Pat the halibut very dry, season both sides with salt and pepper, and let it sit at room temperature while you make the succotash.
Cut the corn kernels off the cobs, halve the tomatoes, dice the shallot, and mince the garlic.
Heat 1 tbsp oil and 1 tbsp butter in a cast-iron skillet over medium-high. Add the corn in an even layer and let it char undisturbed 2–3 minutes, then stir and cook 2 minutes more.
Add the shallot and garlic and cook 1 minute until fragrant. Add the tomatoes and red pepper flakes and cook 2–3 minutes, until the tomatoes slump.
Stir in the basil, lemon juice, salt, and pepper. Scrape into a bowl and wipe out the pan.
Heat 1 tbsp oil in the same pan over medium-high until shimmering. Lay the halibut down presentation-side first and sear undisturbed 3–4 minutes, until deep golden.
Flip, add 1 tbsp butter, and baste 2–3 minutes, until the fish reaches 130°F at the center and flakes. Rest 2 minutes.
Wipe the pan, melt 2 tbsp butter over medium, and swirl until it smells nutty and turns golden-brown, about 2 minutes. Off the heat, stir in the lemon juice and basil.
Spoon the succotash onto plates, set the halibut on top, and pour the brown butter over.

tips: Halibut is lean and dries out fast — pull it at 130°F and let carryover heat finish it to a flaky 135°F off the pan.
Get the fish bone-dry and the pan properly hot before it goes in; that's the entire secret to a golden crust that doesn't stick.
No cast iron? Any heavy stainless pan works — just skip nonstick, which won't build the same crust.
Not at the counter? This works beautifully with cod, sea bass, or swordfish in halibut's place.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Stir garden basil (and a little thyme) into the succotash, and use ripe garden cherry tomatoes for blistering.

extras.label: Off-season swap
extras.note: No sweet summer corn? Frozen kernels, charred hard in the dry pan first, stand in well.

extras.label: Pairs with
extras.note: A crisp white — Sauvignon Blanc or unoaked Chardonnay — and a slab of grilled bread for the brown butter.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
The kind of fast, bright summer plate that feels like a restaurant: a meaty white fish
seared hard, a sweet-and-jammy corn succotash underneath, and brown butter tying it all
together.

recipes.title: Peruvian Pollo a la Brasa with Green Ají Sauce
recipes.tagline: Spatchcocked roast chicken with a deep cumin-paprika marinade and the addictive green ají verde.
recipes.pitch: The roast chicken people drive across town for — and the green sauce that's basically your flavor profile in a blender. A spatchcocked bird gets a cumin-paprika-soy-lime marinade and roasts until the skin lacquers deep mahogany, then it's carved and served with ají verde: jalapeño, cilantro, lime, and garlic whipped silky and spicy-green. *That sauce is the reason this chicken is famous — you'll want to put it on everything.* It's a Sunday-dinner centerpiece that makes its own leftovers.

items: Pat the chicken dry and spatchcock it — remove the backbone with shears and press it flat. Whisk together all the marinade ingredients.
Rub the marinade all over the chicken, including under the skin. Chill at least 4 hours, ideally overnight.

Heat the oven to 425°F. Roast the chicken skin-side up on a rack 45–55 minutes, until deeply bronzed and 165°F in the thigh. Rest 10 minutes before carving. (Or grill over two-zone medium heat, skin-side up over indirect, about 40 minutes.)
Blend all the ají sauce ingredients until smooth and bright green, loosening with a splash of water if needed.
Carve the chicken and serve with the ají verde and lime wedges.

tips: Spatchcocking lets the whole bird cook flat and evenly with maximum crispy skin — use kitchen shears, or have the butcher do it.
Work the marinade under the skin, not just over it, and give it at least 4 hours; overnight is better.
The ají verde is the whole point — make a double batch, it keeps several days and goes on everything.
Heat dial: leave the jalapeño seeds in (or add a serrano) for a real kick; seed them fully for a milder green sauce.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Garden cilantro is the base of the sauce, and a garden jalapeño or serrano dials the heat.

extras.label: Serve with
extras.note: Crispy potatoes or fries and a simple salad — the classic pollería plate.

extras.label: Make it a grill night
extras.note: Two-zone fire, skin-side up over indirect heat with the lid down, for real charcoal char.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
Lima's most famous dish: charcoal-blasted marinated chicken with a creamy, herby, gently
fiery green sauce. The oven gets you most of the way there; the ají verde gets you all the
way.

recipes.title: Poached Cod in Saffron Tomato Broth
recipes.tagline: Cod gently poached in a garlicky, saffron-scented tomato broth.
recipes.pitch: Poaching is the gentlest way to cook cod, and this brothy tomato bath is built for it. Garlic and Aleppo pepper bloom in olive oil, then crushed canned tomatoes, white wine, bay, and a pinch of saffron simmer into a fragrant, faintly golden broth. The cod slips in and cooks at a bare simmer until it's barely opaque and falls into big silky flakes. Serve it in shallow bowls with the broth spooned over and plenty of bread for mopping. *Keep the liquid at a whisper of a simmer — aggressive heat is the only thing that can toughen cod this tender.*

items: ½ tsp Aleppo pepper (or ¼ tsp crushed red pepper flakes)
1 (14.5 oz) can whole peeled tomatoes, drained
Kosher salt and freshly ground black pepper
Heat the oil in a medium skillet over medium heat. Add the garlic and Aleppo pepper and cook, stirring often, until fragrant, about 3 minutes.

Add the tomatoes, crushing them with your hands as you go, along with the wine, bay leaf, saffron, and water. Bring to a boil, then reduce the heat and simmer until the flavors meld, 5–7 minutes. Season with salt and pepper.
Reduce the heat to medium-low. Season the cod with salt and pepper and nestle it into the broth.
Cover and cook at a bare simmer until the cod is opaque throughout and beginning to flake, 5–7 minutes (thicker pieces take longer).
Gently transfer the cod to shallow bowls and spoon the poaching broth over the top.

tips: Saffron blooms best with a little warmth — crumble the threads into the broth as it simmers so the color and aroma fully release.
Any firm white fish works here — halibut, sea bass, or haddock all poach beautifully in this broth.
Serve with grilled or toasted crusty bread; the broth is the whole point and you'll want to mop up every drop.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Finish with torn basil or a little chopped cilantro, and a pinch of fresh jalapeño in the broth if you want more heat.

extras.label: Make it heartier
extras.note: Add a handful of chickpeas or wilt in spinach during the last few minutes for a one-bowl meal.

source.name: Epicurious / Bon Appétit — Alison Roman

recipes.headnote:
Alison Roman's gentle tomato poach: garlic and Aleppo bloomed in oil, a saffron-scented
broth, and cod cooked at a bare simmer until it flakes. Easy, elegant, and built for
crusty bread.

recipes.title: Reverse-Sear New York Strip with Garlic-Herb Broccoli
recipes.tagline: A low-and-slow strip finished with a screaming butter-baste, beside charred garden broccoli.
recipes.pitch: This is the steakhouse trick that takes the guesswork out of a perfect crust. The strip starts in a gentle 250°F oven on a wire rack until it creeps to 115°F, edge-to-edge rosy and dry on the surface — then it hits a smoking cast-iron for a 60-second-a-side sear and a bath of foaming butter, smashed garlic, and garden thyme spooned over the top. Garden broccoli chars hard in the same pan, picking up the drippings and a hit of lemon. *The dry surface is the secret: a steak that's already warm and bone-dry browns in a minute flat, so the inside never overshoots medium-rare.*

items: 2 New York strip steaks (about 1.5 inches thick)
1 large head broccoli, cut into bite-size florets
1 tsp lemon juice, plus zest of ½ lemon
Salt the steaks on all sides and rest on a wire rack at room temperature for at least 40 minutes (or salt the night before, uncovered in the fridge, for the best crust).

Heat the oven to 250°F. Pepper the steaks and set the rack over a baking sheet. Roast until the internal temperature reads 115°F for medium-rare, about 35–45 minutes for 1.5-inch steaks. Use a probe thermometer and start checking early — thinner steaks move fast.
Pull the steaks and rest 5–10 minutes while you heat the pan. The surface should look dry; pat it with a paper towel if not.
Heat a cast-iron skillet over high until just smoking. Add the neutral oil, then lay in the steaks and sear 45–60 seconds per side until a deep crust forms.
Add the butter, smashed garlic, thyme, and rosemary. Tilt the pan and spoon the foaming butter over the steaks for 30 seconds a side. Pull at 125–130°F (it climbs to 130–135°F as it rests).
Rest the steaks 5–10 minutes, topped with a dollop of herb butter if using. Slice against the grain.
Toss the florets with olive oil, salt, pepper, and red pepper flakes. Heat a skillet (or use the steak pan after searing) over medium-high until smoking hot.
Add the broccoli cut-side down in a single layer and char undisturbed 2–3 minutes until deeply browned. Toss and cook 2 more minutes.

Add the sliced garlic, then a splash of water and cover for 1–2 minutes to steam the stems tender. Uncover, let the liquid cook off, and finish with lemon juice and zest. Serve alongside the sliced steak.

tips: The reverse sear's whole advantage is a bone-dry, pre-warmed surface — that's why the steak browns in under a minute without graying the interior. Don't skip the rest before searing.
For a ¾-inch steak, pull from the oven at 110°F (around 20 minutes) and sear hard and fast, 30 seconds a side, or it will sail past medium-rare.
No oven? Bring the steak up gently on the stovetop over low heat with a butter baste, rest, then hard-sear — or sous-vide at 125°F for 45 minutes for edge-to-edge precision.
Let the broccoli sit undisturbed to char; constant stirring steams it gray instead of building those deep brown, caramelized edges.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Garden thyme and rosemary are the heart of the baste; a few sliced cremini or oyster mushrooms sautéed in the steak butter make a rich third element.

extras.label: Make ahead
extras.note: The herb butter can be mixed and refrigerated days in advance; bring to room temperature before topping the steak.

extras.label: Pairs with
extras.note: A structured red — Cabernet Sauvignon or Malbec — and a crusty bread to mop the garlic-herb butter.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
Stephen has run this technique enough times to trust it cold: the gentle oven does
the cooking, and the cast-iron does only the crust. The garden thyme and a hard
char on the broccoli turn a weeknight strip into something that eats like a
steakhouse plate.

recipes.title: Roasted Fennel with Olives & Garlic
recipes.tagline: Fennel wedges roasted until caramelized and sweet with garlic and Kalamata.
recipes.pitch: Roasting is fennel's best friend — the high heat mellows its anise edge into something deep, sweet, and almost candied. Thick wedges go cut-side down on a sheet pan with smashed garlic, briny olives, and a generous slick of olive oil, then roast undisturbed until the flat sides turn burnished and the layers go meltingly tender. The garlic softens to a paste and the olives crisp at the edges. It's a quietly stunning side that asks almost nothing of you. *Leave a little core on each wedge so the layers stay together, then don't move them — that undisturbed contact is what gives you those deep-caramelized faces.*

items: 1 large fennel bulb (about ¾ lb), trimmed and cut into ¾-inch wedges through the core
⅓ cup pitted Kalamata or oil-cured black olives
⅛ tsp freshly ground black pepper
Reserved fennel fronds and a squeeze of lemon, for serving (optional)
On a sheet pan, toss the fennel wedges, garlic, and olives with the olive oil, salt, and pepper. Arrange the fennel cut-side down in a single layer.
Roast for 20 minutes, then flip the wedges to the other cut side and roast 15–20 minutes more, until deeply caramelized and tender when pierced.
Pile onto a platter, scatter with chopped fennel fronds, and finish with a squeeze of lemon if you like.

tips: Don't crowd the pan — give the wedges room so they roast and caramelize instead of steaming.
A pinch of crushed red pepper or fennel seed in the oil deepens the flavor; a little grated Parmesan over the top in the last 5 minutes is excellent if you're not keeping it vegan.
Leftovers are great chopped into a grain bowl or smashed onto toast with the soft roasted garlic.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Finish with chopped fresh thyme or the feathery fennel fronds, and a few torn basil leaves.

extras.label: Pairs with
extras.note: A natural alongside any roasted or broiled fish — it loves a bright, lemony main.

source.name: Epicurious — Molly Stevens

recipes.headnote:
Molly Stevens' approach to roasting fennel: thick wedges, smashed garlic, and olives on
a hot sheet pan until the cut faces caramelize and the layers go silky. A simple,
elegant side that practically cooks itself.

recipes.title: Seared Scallops over Silky Potato Purée with Brown Butter & Charred Lemon
recipes.tagline: Caramel-crusted sea scallops on a buttery potato purée, with nutty brown butter and charred lemon.
recipes.pitch: The date-night showstopper that looks like an afternoon of work and takes thirty minutes. Big dry-packed scallops, patted bone-dry, hit a screaming pan and build a deep caramel crust in two minutes flat over a velvety potato purée whipped silky with butter and cream. *The whole dish is a study in contrast — crackling crust, sweet just-warm center, cool creamy purée underneath.* Brown butter, capers, and a charred lemon pull it together.

items: 1 lb Yukon gold potatoes, peeled and cubed
Cover the potatoes with cold salted water, bring to a boil, and cook until very tender, 15–18 minutes. Drain well and return to the pan over low heat for a minute to steam-dry.
Pass through a ricer (or mash very smooth). Beat in the 4 tbsp butter, then the warm cream, until silky. Season with salt and white pepper and keep warm.
Heat a dry skillet and char the lemon halves cut-side down until deeply browned; set aside.

Pat the scallops bone-dry and season. Heat the oil and 1 tbsp butter in a cast-iron skillet over high until just smoking. Add the scallops without crowding and sear undisturbed 2 minutes, until a deep golden crust forms. Flip and cook 1 minute more, just to opaque. Remove.
Wipe the pan, add the 3 tbsp butter over medium, and swirl until it smells nutty and turns golden-brown. Stir in the capers and, off the heat, a squeeze of charred lemon.
Spoon the purée onto plates, set the scallops on top, pour the brown butter over, and finish with chives and the charred lemon halves.

tips: Dry, dry, dry — pat the scallops bone-dry and don't move them for two minutes; that's the only path to a caramel crust instead of a gray steam.
Buy "dry" (chemical-free) scallops; "wet" ones leak liquid and refuse to brown.
A ricer makes the glossiest purée — and warm the cream and butter before they go in so it stays silky, not gluey.
Swap for Stephen — the purée and brown butter are just as good under a seared halibut or cod fillet.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Chives or a little tarragon stirred into the brown butter; a few thyme leaves over the top.

extras.label: Change the bed
extras.note: Not in a potato mood? A silky sweet-corn or celery-root purée stands in beautifully.

extras.label: Pairs with
extras.note: A glass of Champagne or crisp Chablis — this is a special-occasion plate.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
Shellfish belongs in this book — Lauren loves scallops — and they're the most
restaurant-feeling thing you can make in half an hour. The swap tip keeps Stephen on the
same plate with a fillet of fish.

recipes.title: Serrano-Spiced Chicken with Lemon-Tahini & Date-Almond Pilaf
recipes.tagline: Spice-rubbed chicken over sweet date-almond rice, drizzled with cool lemon-tahini.
recipes.pitch: This plate plays sweet, savory, nutty, and bright all at once. Chicken breasts get a paprika-garlic-serrano rub that perfumes the meat as it sears, laid over a fragrant rice pilaf studded with chopped dates and toasted almonds for little hits of caramel and crunch. Garden peppers run through the whole dish — minced serrano in the rub, sweet banana pepper and jalapeño in the pilaf base, blistered bell pepper strips on top. A cool, lemony tahini drizzle ties it together and cuts the richness. *The dates are the secret — their caramel sweetness against the savory rice and the creamy tahini is what makes this taste like a restaurant dish.*

items: 2 boneless skinless chicken breasts, pounded to even thickness
1 serrano, seeded and minced (from the garden)
1 cup sushi or short-grain rice, rinsed
½ banana pepper, diced (from the garden)
¼ cup almonds, toasted and chopped
3–4 tbsp lemon-tahini sauce (or tahini thinned with lemon juice)

Heat the oven to 425°F. Pound the chicken to an even thickness, then rub all over with paprika, salt, minced garlic, minced serrano, and olive oil. Let it sit at room temperature while you start the rice.
In a saucepan, heat 1 tbsp olive oil and sauté the garlic with the diced banana pepper and jalapeño until soft, about 2 minutes.
Add the rinsed rice and stir to coat, then pour in the hot stock. Cover and cook on very low until tender, about 18 minutes.
Toss the bell pepper and banana pepper strips with olive oil and salt on a sheet pan. Roast at 425°F until blistered and tender, about 10 minutes.
Heat a cast-iron skillet over medium-high. Sear the chicken 5–6 minutes per side until golden and 160°F internal; rest 5 minutes (carryover brings it to 165°F).
Fluff the rice and fold in the chopped dates, toasted almonds, lemon zest, and parsley. Taste and season.
Mound the pilaf, slice the chicken and fan it on top, then drizzle generously with lemon-tahini. Garnish with roasted peppers, diced cucumber, and a lemon wedge.

tips: Mince the serrano fine and work it into the rub — it perfumes the meat from the inside as it sears without turning the dish into a chile challenge.
Pull the breasts at 160°F and rest them; carryover finishes them at 165°F so they stay juicy instead of drying out.
Toast the almonds in a dry pan until they smell nutty before chopping — raw almonds taste flat against the sweet dates.
If your tahini sauce is thick, loosen it with lemon juice and a splash of water until it drizzles in a ribbon.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: This dish is built for the pepper patch — serrano in the rub, banana pepper and jalapeño in the pilaf, and bell pepper roasted on top. Swap in whatever's ripe.

extras.label: Starter
extras.note: Blister extra garden peppers and pile them over whipped goat cheese (mashed with olive oil, a splash of red wine vinegar, and salt) for a quick appetizer.

extras.label: Pairs with
extras.note: A chilled Sauvignon Blanc or Albariño cuts the tahini richness and balances the heat.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
A pantry-raid dinner that turned into a keeper: dates and toasted almonds make
the rice taste Levantine, garden peppers thread heat and sweetness through every
layer, and a cool lemon-tahini drizzle pulls it all together.

recipes.title: Sichuan Spicy Beef Noodle Soup
recipes.tagline: A big-batch mala beef soup — doubanjiang broth, tender beef, fresh noodles, built to order.
recipes.pitch: This is the bowl you crave for years after the first one. Beef browns hard in the Instant Pot, then pressure-cooks into a doubanjiang broth bloomed with Sichuan peppercorn, star anise, and dried chiles until the meat falls apart and the liquid turns red, numbing, and deep. It's built as a big batch on purpose: cook the beef and broth together, then store them apart so you can drop in fresh noodles and blanched bok choy bowl by bowl for days. *The defining move is toasting the doubanjiang until its oil stains the pot red — that bloom is the whole mala backbone, so don't rush it.*

items: 3 lb beef (ideally 1.5 lb short ribs plus 1.5 lb beef shank, or 3 lb chuck)
3–4 inch piece ginger, sliced into ¼-inch coins
6 scallions (whites for broth, greens sliced for garnish)
1 large carrot, cut into thick coins
1½ tsp dried chile flakes (or 6–10 dried chiles)
¼ cup doubanjiang (Pixian chile bean paste)
2 cups water (enough to fill the pot about ⅔ full)
24 oz dried wheat noodles (or 2 lb fresh)
1–2 lb baby bok choy, quartered lengthwise
½–1 tsp white pepper, for finishing
2–3 tsp toasted sesame oil, for drizzling
Table-side: chili oil, Chinkiang black vinegar, pickled mustard greens, extra scallion greens
Pat the beef dry and season all over with fine sea salt. Set the Instant Pot to Sauté (High) and add the oil.
Brown the beef in batches, 3–5 minutes per side, until deeply browned. Crowding steams the meat, so give it room. Transfer to a plate.
Into the hot pot add the scallion whites, ginger, and garlic; stir about 1 minute until fragrant.
Add the Sichuan peppercorns, chile flakes, star anise, bay leaves, and optional fennel; stir 1 minute to bloom.
Add the doubanjiang and stir until it darkens and its oil turns the pot red, 1–2 minutes. Stir in the Shaoxing wine, dark soy, and sugar.
Return the beef and any juices to the pot. Add the carrot coins, then the vegetable broth and water until the pot is about ⅔ full. Stir to dissolve the doubanjiang.
Lock the lid and pressure-cook on High for 55 minutes, then let the pressure release naturally for 20 minutes.
Lift out the beef and dunk it briefly in cool water for about a minute so the surface doesn't dry out, then set on a plate to cool.
Skim most of the fat from the broth, then strain through a fine-mesh strainer into a clean pot. Discard the spices and aromatics.
Remove any bones and slice the beef across the grain into ¼-inch slices, or tear the shank/chuck into ¾–1 inch chunks. At this point the broth and beef can be stored separately up to 5 days.
Boil the noodles to tender-chewy, drain, and rinse briefly to keep them from clumping. Divide among bowls.

Return the strained broth to a simmer with the sliced beef; taste and adjust with salt, a splash more soy, or a teaspoon of sugar to round it out. Add the bok choy to the broth and cook about 1 minute until just tender.

Ladle broth, beef, and bok choy over the noodles. Finish with scallion greens, a pinch of white pepper, and a drizzle of sesame oil. Let each eater tune their bowl with chili oil, black vinegar, and pickled mustard greens.

tips: Toasting the doubanjiang until its oil runs red is the single most important step — that bloom carries the entire mala flavor.
Store the beef and broth separately, and never store cooked noodles in the soup; cook fresh noodles and blanch bok choy each time so leftovers taste restaurant-fresh.
Adjust the mala heat at the table with chili oil rather than the pot, so the base broth stays balanced for everyone.
For Day 2 bowls, a fresh pinch of toasted, ground Sichuan peppercorn restores the numbing tingle the spices lose in storage.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: A handful of garden cilantro and thinly sliced serrano on top brighten the rich broth; swap bok choy for any tender Chinese greens you have on hand.

extras.label: Leftover strategy
extras.note: Freeze broth in 1–2 cup containers or silicone cubes and freeze the beef separately; both keep about 3 months. Reheat broth and beef together to a strong simmer, then add fresh noodles and greens.

extras.label: Pairs with
extras.note: An off-dry Junmai sake or a nigori cuts the chili heat — sweet enough to cool the mala without going dessert-sweet.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
A killer Sichuan bowl Stephen and Lauren have chased for years, scaled up to eight
servings and engineered for leftovers. Cook the beef and broth together, store them
apart, and assemble each bowl to order with fresh noodles, greens, and a table-side
mala station.

recipes.title: Smoky Sous-Vide Chicken Tinga Tacos
recipes.tagline: Silky sous-vide chicken in a glossy chipotle-tomato glaze, crisped and piled into warm tortillas.
recipes.pitch: Tinga, engineered. Chicken breasts go into a 125°F bath with a smoky chipotle-tomato sauce until they're absurdly tender, then get shredded and crisped in a ripping-hot pan so the edges go dark and craggy. The reserved sauce reduces down to a brick-red, spoon-coating glaze that clings to every strand. Pile it into charred tortillas with white onion, cilantro, cotija, and a squeeze of lime, and let quick-pickled red onions cut the richness. *Reduce the sauce until a spoon dragged through leaves a trail that holds for a second — that's peak taco stickiness.*

items: ½ white or yellow onion, sliced
1 (14.5 oz) can crushed or whole tomatoes
1–2 chipotle peppers in adobo (1 for medium, 2 for hot)
Splash of apple cider vinegar or lime juice
1–2 thin slices serrano or jalapeño (from the garden)
Heat the oil in a skillet over medium. Sauté the sliced onion 4–5 minutes until soft and lightly golden, then add the garlic and stir 1 minute.
Add the tomatoes, chipotles, adobo, cumin, smoked paprika, and salt. Simmer 10 minutes, then blend until smooth. Taste — more adobo for heat, a splash of vinegar or lime for brightness.
Season the chicken with the salt, pepper, cumin, smoked paprika, and oregano. Let it sit 10 minutes.
Place the chicken in a sous-vide or zip bag with ⅓ cup of the blended sauce; reserve the rest. Remove the air by water displacement or vacuum sealer.
Cook at 125°F for 1 hour 45 minutes for a silky, juicy texture. (For a more classic firm shred, run 140°F for 90 minutes instead.)

While the chicken cooks, pack the sliced red onion and chile into a jar. Simmer the vinegar, water, sugar, and salt until dissolved, pour over the onions to submerge, and let steep at least 15 minutes.
Pour the reserved sauce into a pan over medium-low and simmer 8–12 minutes until thick, glossy, and spoon-coating. Adjust salt.

Remove the chicken from the bag and shred gently into medium strands (don't over-shred). Heat 1 tbsp oil in a wide skillet over medium-high, spread the chicken in a thin layer, and leave it undisturbed 45–60 seconds to crisp before flipping for another 30 seconds.
Add the reduced sauce and toss to coat; simmer 1–2 minutes just to marry. Stir in the butter off the heat for a silky finish if you like.
Char the tortillas over a flame or in a dry skillet (for flour, skillet-warm then steam under a towel).
Pile in the tinga and top with white onion, cilantro, cotija, pickled onions, and a squeeze of lime.

tips: At 125°F the chicken stays at that temperature far longer than the few minutes needed for pasteurization, so it's chef-safe — but it won't fall apart like braised tinga. Crisping and saucing relaxes the fibers into taco-ready strands.
Make the sauce and season the chicken up to a day ahead, but only bag the chicken with sauce right before it goes in the bath — long acid contact softens the texture too far.
For thin corn tortillas, double them up; saucy tinga will blow through a single layer.
If your tomatoes are very acidic, a teaspoon of honey in the sauce rounds it out.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Drop a thin slice of serrano or jalapeño into the pickling brine for heat, and finish the tacos with a heavy hand of garden cilantro.

extras.label: Crema upgrade
extras.note: Blend ¼ cup crema or sour cream with a roasted jalapeño, lime juice, and salt for a cooling drizzle.

extras.label: Make-ahead
extras.note: The tinga sauce keeps 5 days in the fridge or 3 months frozen, and tastes even better after a day as the aromatics meld.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
Stephen dialed this in over a long thread: sous-vide breasts at 125°F for silk,
a chipotle sauce reduced to a glossy glaze, and a hard crisp in the pan for the
craggy edges that make tinga sing. Quick pickled onions are non-negotiable.

recipes.title: Sous-Vide Salmon with Brown-Butter Citrus Glaze, Charred Asparagus & Herbed Couscous
recipes.tagline: Silky bath-cooked salmon under a nutty brown-butter citrus glaze, with charred asparagus and herbed couscous.
recipes.pitch: This is the refined one — precision and patience over fire. Salmon poaches in a 120°F bath until it's so tender it barely holds a flake, then takes a 30-second kiss in a hot pan for the faintest golden crust. The finish is the showpiece: a beurre noisette swirled with orange and lemon zest until it smells nutty and toasty, spooned warm over the fillet so it pools into the couscous below. *The whole plate turns on that brown butter — pull it the second it smells like hazelnuts and goes amber, because a few seconds more is the difference between nutty and burnt.* Charred asparagus brings the bitter-green contrast.

items: 2 salmon fillets (skin-on or skinless, 5–6 oz each)
Kosher salt and freshly ground black pepper
A pat of butter or drizzle of olive oil (for the bag)
Shaved Parmesan or lemon zest, to finish (optional)
1–2 tbsp chopped fresh herbs (parsley, dill, mint, or basil)
Set the sous-vide to 120°F for medium-rare (125°F for medium). Season the fillets with salt and pepper.
Bag each fillet with a pat of butter or a drizzle of oil, plus thyme and smashed garlic if using. Seal by vacuum or water-displacement.
Submerge and cook 45 minutes (you can hold up to 1 hour without overcooking).
Couscous: bring the broth to a boil, stir in couscous, olive oil, and a pinch of salt. Cover, pull off the heat, and let sit 5 minutes. Fluff, then fold in the shallot and herbs.
Asparagus: toss with olive oil, salt, and pepper and set aside, ready to char.
In a light-colored saucepan, melt the butter over medium and swirl constantly. After 3–5 minutes it foams, then turns amber with golden flecks and a nutty aroma — pull it immediately.
Off the heat, stir in the orange and lemon zest, 1–2 tsp of each juice, the honey, and capers if using. Keep warm.
Sear the asparagus in a hot pan or grill pan until lightly charred and crisp-tender. Pat the salmon dry and sear presentation-side down 30–60 seconds for a golden crust.
Spoon couscous onto each plate, add the asparagus, and set the salmon alongside. Drizzle warm brown-butter glaze over the fish and finish with a little extra zest.

tips: Brown butter turns on a dime — use a light-colored pan so you can see the color change, and have your zest and juice measured before you start.
Pour the finished brown butter into a warm bowl off the heat; the residual pan heat will keep darkening it otherwise.
Toast the dry couscous in a dry pan for a minute before adding broth for a deeper, nuttier flavor.
The sear is for color and aroma only — 30 to 60 seconds. The fish is already cooked, so any longer and you lose the silky bath texture.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Finish the couscous with garden mint or basil, and a stir of chopped thyme into the brown butter echoes the herbs in the bag.

extras.label: Add crunch
extras.note: Toast a tablespoon of pine nuts and fold them into the couscous, or scatter them over the asparagus for texture.

extras.label: Pairs with
extras.note: A chilled white with some weight — Chardonnay, Viognier, or a dry Riesling stands up to the brown butter.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
The elegant entry in the salmon rotation: sous-vide for an unbroken, custardy
flake, then a beurre noisette brightened with orange and lemon. The original lemon-
butter-caper idea grew into a proper brown-butter glaze, and the herbed couscous
and charred asparagus turn it into a full, restaurant-grade plate for two.

recipes.title: Soy-Hoisin Steak Kebabs
recipes.tagline: Sticky-glazed sirloin skewers with sweet peppers and onion, charred over a hot grill.
recipes.pitch: These skewers earn their lacquer. Cubes of sirloin soak overnight in soy, hoisin, honey, and toasted sesame until they're savory-sweet to the core, then get threaded between garden bell peppers and red onion and grilled hard until the edges char and the glaze turns sticky. The hoisin caramelizes against the live fire for a glossy, almost barbecue-like crust. *Reduce the leftover marinade to a quick glaze and brush it on in the last minute — that's the lacquered, restaurant shine.*

items: ½ tsp freshly ground black pepper
¼ tsp red pepper flakes (optional, for heat)
1 lb sirloin steak, cut into 1.5-inch cubes
1 red bell pepper, cut into 1.5-inch squares
1 green bell pepper, cut into 1.5-inch squares
1 tbsp toasted sesame seeds, for garnish
Whisk all the marinade ingredients in a bowl until smooth. Reserve ¼ cup for glazing later.
Add the steak cubes to the remaining marinade, tossing to coat. Cover and refrigerate at least 2 hours, ideally overnight.
If using wooden skewers, soak them in water for 30 minutes so they don't scorch.
Thread the steak onto skewers, alternating with bell pepper and red onion. Leave a little space between pieces so they char rather than steam.
Preheat the grill to medium-high and oil the grates. Grill the kebabs 8–10 minutes, turning every 2–3 minutes, until the steak is medium-rare (130–135°F) and the vegetables are charred and tender.
In the last minute, brush the skewers with the reserved marinade for a sticky, glossy glaze.
Rest the kebabs 5 minutes. Scatter with toasted sesame seeds and sliced scallions and serve over rice.

tips: Always reserve a portion of the marinade before the raw steak goes in — that clean portion is what you brush on at the end and use as a finishing glaze.
Cut the steak, peppers, and onion to the same size so everything cooks at the same rate on the skewer.
Hoisin and honey both have sugar, so they burn fast; glaze only in the final minute and keep the kebabs moving over the heat.
Leave a small gap between pieces on the skewer — crowding traps steam and prevents a good char.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Add chunks of garden zucchini or cherry tomatoes to the skewers, and stir a little minced serrano or jalapeño into the marinade for a sharper kick.

extras.label: Make ahead
extras.note: The steak can marinate up to 24 hours; thread the skewers in the morning and refrigerate so they're grill-ready at dinner.

extras.label: Pairs with
extras.note: Steamed jasmine rice and a cucumber salad, with a chilled off-dry Riesling or a light lager.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
The source was just a marinade, but it's a good one — soy and hoisin doing the
heavy lifting, sesame and ginger rounding it out. Built into proper skewers with
garden peppers and a last-minute glaze, it eats like takeout you'd actually pay
for.

recipes.title: Sriracha-Honey Salmon with Crispy Kale Chips
recipes.tagline: Sous-vide salmon lacquered in sweet-heat glaze, grill-charred, with shattering spiced kale.
recipes.pitch: This is the sweet-heat lacquer move: salmon goes into the bath at a precise 125°F until it's custard-tender all the way through, then hits a screaming grill for ninety seconds a side — just long enough to caramelize a honey-and- Sriracha glaze into glassy, sticky char without pushing the inside past medium. Alongside, curly kale roasts low and slow with garlic salt, smoked paprika, and cayenne until the leaves shatter like edible solar panels. *Because the sous-vide locks the protein first, you can glaze and char the surface aggressively while the interior stays silky.* It tastes engineered, and it basically is.

items: 2 salmon fillets, 5–7 oz each, skin removed
½ tsp rice vinegar (optional, brightens the glaze)
Kosher salt (a light season before bagging)
1 bunch curly kale, stems removed, torn into 2-inch pieces
Pinch of brown sugar (optional, for better browning)
Whisk honey, Sriracha, soy, sesame oil, garlic, ginger, and optional rice vinegar. Reserve 1 tbsp to brush on after grilling.
Set the sous-vide to 125°F. Lightly salt the fillets, bag each with 1–2 tsp of glaze (just a touch — too much dilutes the sear), and seal.
Submerge and cook 45 minutes. You can hold up to 15 minutes longer without overcooking.
Heat the oven to 300°F. Toss kale with olive oil, garlic salt, paprika, cayenne, and the optional brown sugar.
Spread in a single layer with no overlapping leaves — airflow is what makes them crisp.
Roast 12–15 minutes, rotate the tray, then 3–5 minutes more until shatter-crisp. Watch the last few minutes closely; they go from crisp to scorched fast. Finish with a squeeze of lime if you like.
Heat the grill to high; clean and oil the grates. Remove the salmon from the bag and pat gently dry.
Brush a thin layer of glaze over the fillets and grill 1–2 minutes per side, lid open — you want char marks and glassy lacquer, not more interior cooking.
Brush with the reserved glaze off-heat. Plate with a pile of kale chips alongside and drizzle any extra glaze over the top.

tips: 125°F gives a buttery, restaurant medium-rare that's fully pasteurized over 45 minutes — firm enough to survive the grill without breaking apart.
Keep the bag glaze minimal; surface moisture is the enemy of caramelization. Save the real glaze hit for the grill and the finish.
Honey scorches quickly over direct flame, so keep the glaze thin and the grill time short — lacquer, then pull.
If you'd rather not fire the grill, broil the patted-dry fillets 2–3 inches from the element for 2–3 minutes, glazing once, until the surface blisters.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: A squeeze of garden lime and a scatter of torn cilantro over the finished plate cut the sweetness and add a fresh, herby lift.

extras.label: Make it gluten-free
extras.note: Swap the soy sauce for tamari or coconut aminos; everything else is already gluten-free.

extras.label: Heat dial
extras.note: Push the cayenne and Sriracha for a serious burn, or pull both back and lean on the smoked paprika for a milder, smokier glaze.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
A NASA-flavor-division build that Stephen dialed in across a few rounds: sous-vide
sets the protein at a precise 125°F so the grill can do nothing but char and
lacquer. The kale keeps his garlic-salt, paprika, and cayenne profile but roasts
low to stay crisp and bitter-free — a savory, crackling foil to the sticky
sweet-heat fish.

recipes.title: Steak au Poivre with Cognac-Peppercorn Cream
recipes.tagline: A peppercorn-crusted steak seared in cast iron, finished with a silky cognac-cream pan sauce.
recipes.pitch: This is the French-bistro classic worth learning for the sauce alone: a steak pressed in coarsely cracked black pepper, seared hard in cast iron, then set aside while you build a pan sauce on its fond. Cognac goes in to deglaze — flamed or simmered off — and reduces with broth and cream into something glossy and peppery. *Igniting the cognac burns off its raw edge and leaves a deep, toasty backbone in the sauce — a thirty-second piece of theater.* It eats like a restaurant and teaches you a proper pan sauce in one go.

items: 2 filet mignon or New York strip steaks (about 6 oz each)
Pat the steaks very dry and season all over with salt. Crack the peppercorns coarsely (rock a heavy pan over them on the board) and press them onto both flat sides of each steak.
Heat the oil and 1 tbsp butter in a cast-iron skillet over medium-high. Sear the steaks 3–4 minutes per side, to 125°F for medium-rare. Move them to a plate to rest.

Lower the heat to medium and cook the shallot in the pan drippings 1 minute. Pull the pan off the heat, add the cognac, then return it — and either flame it off with a long lighter or simmer 1 minute to cook off the alcohol — scraping up the fond.
Add the beef broth and reduce by half. Stir in the cream and Dijon and simmer until the sauce coats a spoon, 2–3 minutes. Off the heat, swirl in the butter and season with salt.
Return the steaks and any resting juices to the pan, spoon the sauce over, and serve.

tips: Crack the peppercorns coarse, not fine — you want fragrant, crunchy cracks, not a dusty burn.
Flambé safely: pull the pan off the heat before the cognac goes in, then ignite with a long lighter (or just simmer it off) — keep your face and the hood clear.
Pull the steaks at 125°F; they climb to a perfect medium-rare while the sauce comes together.
No cognac? Brandy or bourbon works, and a broth-and-cream sauce with no spirit is still excellent.

extras.label: Pairs with
extras.note: Crispy frites or a pile of dressed watercress — the full bistro plate.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: A little chopped thyme stirred into the sauce, or a tangle of garden watercress alongside.

extras.label: Make it richer
extras.note: A spoonful of brined green peppercorns stirred into the sauce doubles the pepper character.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
The dish that taught a generation of home cooks how to build a pan sauce — sear, deglaze,
reduce, mount with butter — using nothing but the brown bits left in the pan.

recipes.title: Summer Caprese Pasta with Roasted Squash Ribbons & Burst Tomatoes
recipes.tagline: Spaghetti tangled with charred squash ribbons, jammy tomatoes, basil, and torn mozzarella.
recipes.pitch: This is a garden party in a bowl — ribbons of yellow squash roasted until their edges caramelize, cherry tomatoes blistered until they burst and release their sweet juices, all tossed with spaghetti and torn fresh basil. The warm pasta softens cool pearls of mozzarella just enough to go molten at the edges, and a homemade balsamic glaze, reduced down to a syrup, lands the sweet-tart finish. It's the warm-and-cool contrast that makes it sing — hot, glossy noodles against creamy cheese and bright herbs. *Shave the squash thin with a Y-peeler and roast the ribbons just long enough to brown the edges, never long enough to go soggy — that caramelized edge is the whole dish.*

items: 1 medium yellow squash (or half yellow squash, half zucchini)
2 cups cherry tomatoes (mixed colors if you have them)
Kosher salt and freshly ground black pepper
1 tsp honey or sugar (optional, for gloss)
¾–1 cup mozzarella pearls (or 1 ball fresh mozzarella, torn)
In a small saucepan over medium heat, bring the balsamic vinegar to a gentle simmer and stir in the honey if using.

Simmer, stirring occasionally, until reduced by half and thick enough to coat the back of a spoon, 8–10 minutes. Watch it closely near the end so it doesn't burn; lower the heat if it bubbles hard. Set aside to cool and thicken.
Preheat the oven to 425°F. Using a Y-peeler or mandoline, shave the squash into thin ribbons.

On a parchment-lined sheet pan, toss the squash ribbons with olive oil, salt, and pepper and spread them on one half in a single layer. On the other half, toss the tomatoes with olive oil, sliced garlic, salt, and pepper.
Roast 10–12 minutes, until the tomatoes blister and burst and the squash edges turn golden.
Meanwhile, bring a large pot of salted water to a boil and cook the spaghetti to al dente. Reserve ½ cup pasta water, then drain.

In a large bowl or wide sauté pan, combine the hot pasta, roasted tomatoes with all their juices, and the squash ribbons. Add 2–3 tbsp olive oil and a splash of pasta water and toss until glossy and loose.
Stir in the torn basil and red pepper flakes if using; add more pasta water if it looks dry.
Twirl into shallow bowls, nestle in the mozzarella so it softens from the heat, drizzle generously with the balsamic glaze, and finish with more basil and cracked black pepper.

tips: Roast the squash just until the edges brown — pull it before it slumps, or the ribbons go limp and watery in the bowl.
Add the mozzarella off the heat at the very end so it stays in soft, molten pockets instead of dissolving into the sauce.
This is just as good at room temperature — let it cool and it becomes a stellar summer pasta salad for the next day.
No balsamic glaze on hand? The reduction here takes ten minutes; or blend basil, olive oil, and a pinch of salt into a quick basil oil instead.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: This is peak-tomato food — use garden cherry and grape tomatoes for color and sweetness, and a generous handful of garden basil torn in at the end.

extras.label: Make it more filling
extras.note: Add a spoon of ricotta stirred through, a swirl of pesto, or toasted pine nuts and pesto breadcrumbs for crunch.

extras.label: Pairs with
extras.note: A crisp white like Vermentino or Albariño and a slab of grilled sourdough brushed with olive oil.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
Stephen circled through a lot of summer pasta pitches — scallops, peaches,
puttanesca — before settling on the squash-tomato-basil-mozzarella combination, a
true summer classic, then scaling it for two and folding in a homemade balsamic
glaze. Meatless, garden-forward, and best when the tomatoes are at their peak.

recipes.title: Thai Grilled Beef "Crying Tiger" Salad (Nam Tok)
recipes.tagline: Charred grilled beef sliced thin over herbs in a fierce lime, fish sauce, and toasted-rice dressing.
recipes.pitch: This is the Thai grilled-beef salad that hits four things at once — smoke, herbs, sour-salty heat, and a nutty grip you can't quite place. A marinated steak chars hard over the grill, rests, and gets sliced thin, then tossed warm with mint, cilantro, shallot, and a dressing of lime, fish sauce, and chili. *The secret is khao khua — raw rice toasted dark and ground to a powder — which gives the dressing a nutty texture that clings to every slice.* It's bright, fiery, and built around the grill you already love.

items: 12 oz flank, skirt, or sirloin steak
1 tsp red pepper flakes (or 1–2 Thai chiles, minced)
4 cups butter lettuce or mixed greens
Whisk the fish sauce, soy, oil, brown sugar, garlic, and white pepper. Coat the steak and marinate 30 minutes at room temperature (or up to 4 hours, chilled).
If you don't have toasted rice powder: toast 2 tbsp raw rice in a dry pan over medium until deep golden and fragrant, 5–8 minutes, then grind to a coarse powder.
Heat the grill to high and oil the grates. Grill the steak 3–4 minutes per side, until charred outside and 130°F for medium-rare.
Rest the steak 5 minutes, then slice thinly against the grain.
Stir together the fish sauce, lime juice, brown sugar, toasted rice powder, and chili until the sugar dissolves.
Add the warm sliced beef, shallot, scallions, mint, and cilantro and toss to coat.
Bed the lettuce with cucumber and tomatoes, pile the dressed beef and herbs on top, and spoon over any dressing left in the bowl.

tips: Toast raw rice until it's the color of dark caramel before grinding — that nutty khao khua is the soul of the dressing, not an optional garnish.
Grill to a true medium-rare (130°F) and slice thin against the grain so the beef stays tender against the crunchy herbs.
Dress the beef while it's still warm; it drinks up the lime and fish sauce far better than cold.
Heat dial: 1 tsp flakes is a gentle hum — go to 2–3 fresh Thai chiles for a real crying-tiger burn.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Garden mint and cilantro are the entire herb bill; thinly sliced serranos stand in nicely for the Thai chiles.

extras.label: Serve with
extras.note: Warm sticky rice or jasmine rice to temper the heat and soak up the dressing.

extras.label: Make it a spread
extras.note: Set out extra dressing as a "jaew" dipping sauce alongside the sliced beef.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
Nam tok means "waterfall" — for the juices that drip off the grilling beef. It's the
livelier, herb-packed cousin of a steak salad, and the toasted rice is what makes people
ask what's in it.

recipes.title: Tilapia Piccata with Fettuccine & Broccoli
recipes.tagline: Crisp-seared tilapia in a glossy lemon-caper pan sauce over fettuccine and charred broccoli.
recipes.pitch: This is chicken piccata's lighter, faster cousin — delicate tilapia gets a whisper-thin flour dredge so it crisps golden without going gummy, leaving a fond that becomes the backbone of the sauce. Deglaze with white wine, simmer down lemon juice and briny capers, then swirl in cold butter off the heat until the sauce goes glossy and clings. The same skillet chars broccoli florets so they drink up the drippings, and the fettuccine finishes right in the pan with a splash of starchy water. *The trick is the off-heat butter swirl — monter au beurre — that turns sharp lemon and wine into a silky emulsion that coats every noodle.*

items: Kosher salt and freshly cracked black pepper
¼ cup all-purpose flour (for a micro-dredge)
½ cup dry white wine (Sauvignon Blanc or Pinot Grigio), or low-sodium chicken stock
2 tbsp capers, drained (smash a few lightly)
1 large head broccoli, cut into florets (peel and slice the stem)
Pat the tilapia completely dry, season both sides with salt and pepper, then dredge lightly in flour and shake off the excess — a thin coat means a crisp crust and a clean, glossy sauce.
Cut the broccoli into florets; peel the stem and slice it thin to sear alongside. Toss with olive oil, salt, pepper, and chili flakes if using.
Mince the garlic, slice the lemon into thin wheels, juice the remaining lemon, chop the parsley, and drain the capers (smash a few with the flat of your knife for extra brine).
Bring a large pot of well-salted water to a boil and cook the fettuccine to al dente. Reserve ½ cup pasta water, then drain and toss with a little olive oil to keep it loose.

Meanwhile, heat a large skillet over medium-high. Add a drizzle of oil and the broccoli in a single layer; leave it untouched 2–3 minutes to char, toss once, sear 1 minute more, then scoot it to the side of the pan or remove to a bowl.

Add the 2 tbsp olive oil and 1 tbsp butter to the cleared pan. Lay in the tilapia and sear undisturbed about 3 minutes until golden, flip, and cook 2–3 minutes more until it just flakes. Transfer to a warm plate.
Lower the heat to medium. Add the garlic and cook 20–30 seconds until fragrant, then lay in the lemon slices and let them caramelize 30–45 seconds per side.
Pour in the wine (or stock) and scrape up the fond. Add the capers and reduce by about half, 1–2 minutes. Stir in the lemon juice.
Off the heat, swirl in the 2 tbsp finishing butter a pat at a time until the sauce turns glossy, then stir in the parsley. Taste and adjust salt and acid.
Add the drained fettuccine and broccoli to the sauce, splashing in pasta water as needed until everything is silky and coated.
Nest the pasta and broccoli into shallow bowls, lay the tilapia on top, and spoon the remaining sauce over the fish.

tips: Pull the tilapia the instant it flakes — it is delicate, and carryover heat finishes it on the plate. Overcooked, it turns chalky.
Searing the lemon slices caramelizes their acidity into a deeper, rounder citrus flavor; don't skip them in the sauce.
Reserve more pasta water than you think you need — its starch is what makes the butter-wine sauce cling to the noodles instead of pooling.
If the sauce tastes too sharp at the end, swirl in one more pat of cold butter; too flat, add a final squeeze of lemon.

extras.label: From the garden
extras.note: Finish with extra chopped parsley and a little fresh thyme, and brighten the sauce with garden lemon zest grated in just before serving.

extras.label: Pairs with
extras.note: A crisp, unoaked white — Sauvignon Blanc or Pinot Grigio — echoes the wine in the sauce. Add grilled bread to mop up the extra.

extras.label: Make it greener
extras.note: Swap or supplement the broccoli with broccolini or asparagus tips, charred the same way in the skillet.

source.name: Adapted from Stephen's ChatGPT recipe notes

recipes.headnote:
Stephen worked this one through a couple of rounds — first sorting out a rogue
lemon slice that kept appearing from nowhere, then folding the fettuccine cleanly
into the sauce. The result eats like a restaurant lemon-caper trio: crisp fish,
silky sauce, and deeply seared broccoli, all in one skillet.

recipes.title: Tomato-Basil Chicken Fettuccine with Fresh Mozzarella
recipes.tagline: Seared chicken and a quick fresh-tomato sauce tossed with fettuccine and melty mozzarella.
recipes.pitch: This is the weeknight pasta that tastes like late summer. Chicken sears golden in olive oil, then fresh tomatoes cook down in the same pan into a loose, jammy sauce flecked with garlic and a whisper of chili. Fettuccine goes in to catch it all, and cubes of fresh mozzarella melt into soft, creamy pockets as you toss. Torn basil and an optional thread of balsamic at the end keep it bright instead of heavy. *Stir the mozzarella in off the heat so it goes molten and stretchy rather than tough — those creamy pockets are the whole point.*

items: 2 small chicken breasts (or 1 large), sliced into thin strips
2 large or 4 medium fresh tomatoes, diced
4 oz fresh mozzarella, cut into small cubes
A handful of fresh basil, torn (from the garden)
Kosher salt and freshly ground black pepper
Bring a large pot of salted water to a boil. Cook the fettuccine until just al dente, then reserve ½ cup pasta water and drain.

Season the chicken strips with salt and pepper. Heat 1–2 tbsp olive oil in a large skillet over medium-high and cook the chicken 3–5 minutes per side until golden and cooked through. Remove and set aside.
Lower the heat to medium and add a little more oil if needed. Sauté the garlic 1–2 minutes until fragrant and just golden — don't let it burn.
Add the diced tomatoes with a pinch of salt, pepper, and chili flakes if using. Cook 5–7 minutes, stirring, until they break down into a slightly chunky sauce.
Return the chicken to the pan and toss to coat. Add the drained fettuccine, loosening with a splash of pasta water until the sauce clings.
